text:
1	---
     2	status: decided
     3	created: 2026-07-17
     4	scope: Define and test the smallest useful Hax agent harness against real job fixtures
     5	builds_on:
     6	  - discussion/001-hax-goal-and-run-lifecycle.md
     7	  - discussion/002-durable-work-and-continuity.md
     8	uses:
     9	  - docs/harness-primitives/
    10	  - discussion/fixtures/003-five-job-sample.md
    11	replaces_active_direction:
    12	  - discussion/003-company-watch-vertical-slice.md
    13	---
    14	
    15	# Minimal job-native harness
    16	
    17	## Course correction
    18	
    19	The previous discussion took a useful company-watch example and started designing a large product domain before we had watched Hax solve even one real job problem.
    20	
    21	That was backwards.
    22	
    23	The company-watch scenario should be an evaluation case for the agent system, not an excuse to settle every future object, table, workflow, trigger, and delivery rule first.
    24	
    25	The better method follows the harness lessons in `docs/harness-primitives/`:
    26	
    27	```text
    28	start with one model call
    29	-> add the smallest surrounding code
    30	-> run one real task
    31	-> save the full trace and output
    32	-> identify the exact failure
    33	-> add one missing primitive or domain rule
    34	-> run the same task again
    35	-> keep only changes that improve the result
    36	```
    37	
    38	The model stays behind one stable boundary. HaxJobs grows around it from observed failures rather than imagined completeness.
    39	
    40	## The central question
    41	
    42	We are not trying to design the whole job-search product first.
    43	
    44	We are trying to answer:
    45	
    46	> What is the smallest piece of software around a model that makes it behave like Hax, a truthful employment agent, rather than a generic chatbot or coding agent?
    47	
    48	Once that small system exists, real job fixtures will teach us what context, tools, commands, workflows, skills, saved state, and checks it actually needs.
    49	
    50	## Where the user's correction is right
    51	
    52	The correction is right in three important ways:
    53	
    54	1. **HaxJobs is the agent harness. Hax is the agent produced by that system.** The product objects exist to support Hax's work, not the other way around.
    55	2. **The harness primitives are the design frame.** Instructions, context, tools, state, execution, control, verification, and traces must be shaped around employment work.
    56	3. **Real runs should drive the architecture.** We should observe Hax handling actual jobs, then fix concrete failures one at a time.
    57	
    58	## One correction to the correction
    59	
    60	We still need a tiny test contract before writing code.
    61	
    62	"Build a minimal harness" without a defined job task and pass criteria would produce a generic tool loop with HaxJobs names. We need one real user request, one candidate context fixture, one job fixture, and a short evaluation checklist.
    63	
    64	That is not heavyweight product design. It is the minimum needed to tell whether the next run got better or merely sounded different.
    65	
    66	# What makes a job-native harness different
    67	
    68	A job-native system does not need different laws of agent engineering. It uses the same twelve primitives as a coding agent, but the centre of gravity changes.
    69	
    70	Claude Code is built around a repository, files, shell commands, code changes, and tests.
    71	
    72	HaxJobs is built around a person, career evidence, live job and company information, decisions, applications, outcomes, and external actions that can affect the person's career.
    73	
    74	## Shared spine
    75	
    76	Both systems still need:
    77	
    78	```text
    79	interface
    80	-> harness assembles instructions, context, and allowed actions
    81	-> model chooses the next move
    82	-> harness validates and runs allowed code
    83	-> result returns to the model
    84	-> loop stops with an answer or controlled pause
    85	-> trace and useful state are saved
    86	```
    87	
    88	The difference is what each primitive protects and optimizes.
    89	
    90	## Job-native translation of the twelve primitives
    91	
    92	| Primitive | Coding-agent centre | HaxJobs centre |
    93	|---|---|---|
    94	| Instructions | Repository rules, coding style, safe edits | Get the user interviews and hired without inventing evidence, job facts, or consent |
    95	| Context delivery | Relevant files, symbols, errors, git diff | Current user request, one career direction, relevant evidence, job source snapshot, company facts, prior decisions |
    96	| Context management | Keep logs and file dumps out of the window | Keep irrelevant career history, stale jobs, unsupported claims, and unrelated tracks out of the run |
    97	| Tools | Read, edit, shell, tests | Inspect a job source, retrieve career evidence, research a company, save a decision, prepare an approved career action |
    98	| Execution environment | Filesystem, subprocess, network, secrets | Personal-data scope, source trust, provider credentials, cost, and approval for sending, contacting, or submitting |
    99	| Durable state | Session, workspace, code changes | Career facts, evidence provenance, job observations, decisions, outcomes, commitments, and conversation continuity |
   100	| Orchestration | Plan edits, run tests, retry commands | Move from lead to verified role to fit judgement to user decision without skipping evidence or approval |
   101	| Sub-agents | Isolated code research or implementation | Focused company research, recruiter research, interview simulation, or parallel source inspection when later justified |
   102	| Skills | Repeatable coding procedure | Repeatable job-search procedure proven useful across real runs |
   103	| Verification | Test, typecheck, artifact receipt | Current source evidence, identity, claim provenance, liveness, saved decision, and exact approval receipt |
   104	| Observability | Model calls, tool calls, code changes, tests | Which user facts and job evidence Hax saw, what it inferred, what it could not verify, actions, cost, and final answer |
   105	| Evolution | Fix recurring coding-agent failure | Turn repeated bad fit calls, missing evidence, noisy updates, or source mistakes into tested harness changes |
   106	
   107	## The biggest job-native differences
   108	
   109	### 1. The person is the long-lived subject
   110	
   111	A coding agent usually starts from the current repository state. Hax starts from a person whose goals, evidence, constraints, and history continue across jobs.
   112	
   113	The harness must select the right slice of that person for the current task. Dumping a giant profile into every prompt would be the career equivalent of dumping an entire repository into Claude Code.
   114	
   115	### 2. Truth has two sides
   116	
   117	Hax compares:
   118	
   119	- claims about the user
   120	- claims about the job or company
   121	
   122	Both sides need sources and dates. A confident overlap between two unsupported claims is still unsupported.
   123	
   124	### 3. Time matters more
   125	
   126	A code file normally remains the same until someone edits it. Jobs close, careers pages change, work authorization changes, user priorities move, and hiring claims become stale.
   127	
   128	Currentness and observation time are part of the evidence, not optional metadata.
   129	
   130	### 4. The final answer can change real life
   131	
   132	A wrong code suggestion may fail a test. A wrong career claim can waste hours, expose private information, damage trust, or cause a dishonest application.
   133	
   134	Hax needs a clear difference between:
   135	
   136	- discussing
   137	- researching
   138	- saving internal state
   139	- drafting an external action
   140	- causing an external effect
   141	
   142	### 5. Success is not one deterministic test command
   143	
   144	A job assessment cannot be proven by `pytest` alone.
   145	
   146	We need several kinds of checking:
   147	
   148	- deterministic checks for source identity, schemas, and saved state
   149	- evidence checks for material claims
   150	- behavioural evaluations for model judgement
   151	- later outcome evidence from replies, interviews, offers, and rejections
   152	
   153	The first experiment needs only the first three.
   154	
   155	# The first harness experiment
   156	
   157	## Goal
   158	
   159	Build the smallest Hax that can answer one real question:
   160	
   161	> "What do you think of this role? Is it worth my time?"
   162	
   163	It receives one frozen career-context fixture and one real job fixture. It must return a conversational answer while staying honest about evidence and uncertainty.
   164	
   165	## Why start here instead of company watching
   166	
   167	Watching adds scheduling, repeated checks, source changes, deduplication, delivery, and durable commitments before we know whether Hax can reason about one job properly.
   168	
   169	The thin job-review task tests the central job-native loop first:
   170	
   171	```text
   172	user question
   173	+ selected career context
   174	+ job evidence
   175	-> model judgement
   176	-> optional source inspection
   177	-> evidence-bearing answer
   178	-> saved trace
   179	```
   180	
   181	Once this works, company watching can repeatedly feed new jobs into the same review capability.
   182	
   183	## Fixture order
   184	
   185	The existing fixture contains five real records with useful defects.
   186	
   187	### First run: Job 49, Trainline IT Support Analyst
   188	
   189	Why first:
   190	
   191	- it has a substantial job description
   192	- the correct broad decision should be easy
   193	- it tests whether Hax follows the user's career direction rather than matching random technical words
   194	- it tests whether Hax refuses to treat unsupported sponsorship data as fact
   195	- it gives us a clean control case before adding web research
   196	
   197	Expected broad behaviour:
   198	
   199	- recognise that this is internal IT support, not backend or AI engineering
   200	- explain the mismatch using actual job responsibilities
   201	- avoid being distracted by mentions of automation, AI, Azure, or Bash
   202	- avoid claiming sponsorship is safe or unsafe without evidence
   203	- tell the user it is probably not worth their time
   204	- stay conversational rather than dumping a scoring report
   205	
   206	### Second run: Job 328, Oritain Software Engineer
   207	
   208	Why second:
   209	
   210	- the title looks promising
   211	- the stored record contains only a title and LinkedIn URL
   212	- the old evaluation confidently invented or imported detail that the stored evidence cannot support
   213	- it forces Hax to recognise insufficient evidence
   214	- it tells us whether source-inspection should become the first real tool
   215	
   216	Expected broad behaviour before a source tool exists:
   217	
   218	- say there is not enough verified job information for a proper fit judgement
   219	- avoid repeating Django, FastAPI, TypeScript, cloud, company mission, or sponsorship claims unless the current run received evidence for them
   220	- identify what needs to be checked next

     3	created: 2026-07-17
     4	scope: Define and test the smallest useful Hax agent harness against real job fixtures
     5	builds_on:
     6	  - discussion/001-hax-goal-and-run-lifecycle.md
     7	  - discussion/002-durable-work-and-continuity.md
     8	uses:
     9	  - docs/harness-primitives/
    10	  - discussion/fixtures/003-five-job-sample.md
    11	replaces_active_direction:
    12	  - discussion/003-company-watch-vertical-slice.md
    13	---
    14	
    15	# Minimal job-native harness
    16	
    17	## Course correction
    18	
    19	The previous discussion took a useful company-watch example and started designing a large product domain before we had watched Hax solve even one real job problem.
    20	
    21	That was backwards.
    22	
    23	The company-watch scenario should be an evaluation case for the agent system, not an excuse to settle every future object, table, workflow, trigger, and delivery rule first.
    24	
    25	The better method follows the harness lessons in `docs/harness-primitives/`:
    26	
    27	```text
    28	start with one model call
    29	-> add the smallest surrounding code
    30	-> run one real task
    31	-> save the full trace and output
    32	-> identify the exact failure
    33	-> add one missing primitive or domain rule
    34	-> run the same task again
    35	-> keep only changes that improve the result
    36	```
    37	
    38	The model stays behind one stable boundary. HaxJobs grows around it from observed failures rather than imagined completeness.
    39	
    40	## The central question
    41	
    42	We are not trying to design the whole job-search product first.
    43	
    44	We are trying to answer:
    45	
    46	> What is the smallest piece of software around a model that makes it behave like Hax, a truthful employment agent, rather than a generic chatbot or coding agent?
    47	
    48	Once that small system exists, real job fixtures will teach us what context, tools, commands, workflows, skills, saved state, and checks it actually needs.
    49	
    50	## Where the user's correction is right
    51	
    52	The correction is right in three important ways:
    53	
    54	1. **HaxJobs is the agent harness. Hax is the agent produced by that system.** The product objects exist to support Hax's work, not the other way around.
    55	2. **The harness primitives are the design frame.** Instructions, context, tools, state, execution, control, verification, and traces must be shaped around employment work.
    56	3. **Real runs should drive the architecture.** We should observe Hax handling actual jobs, then fix concrete failures one at a time.
    57	
    58	## One correction to the correction
    59	
    60	We still need a tiny test contract before writing code.
    61	
    62	"Build a minimal harness" without a defined job task and pass criteria would produce a generic tool loop with HaxJobs names. We need one real user request, one candidate context fixture, one job fixture, and a short evaluation checklist.
    63	
    64	That is not heavyweight product design. It is the minimum needed to tell whether the next run got better or merely sounded different.
    65	
    66	# What makes a job-native harness different
    67	
    68	A job-native system does not need different laws of agent engineering. It uses the same twelve primitives as a coding agent, but the centre of gravity changes.
    69	
    70	Claude Code is built around a repository, files, shell commands, code changes, and tests.
    71	
    72	HaxJobs is built around a person, career evidence, live job and company information, decisions, applications, outcomes, and external actions that can affect the person's career.
    73	
    74	## Shared spine
    75	
    76	Both systems still need:
    77	
    78	```text
    79	interface
    80	-> harness assembles instructions, context, and allowed actions
    81	-> model chooses the next move
    82	-> harness validates and runs allowed code
    83	-> result returns to the model
    84	-> loop stops with an answer or controlled pause
    85	-> trace and useful state are saved
    86	```
    87	
    88	The difference is what each primitive protects and optimizes.
    89	
    90	## Job-native translation of the twelve primitives
    91	
    92	| Primitive | Coding-agent centre | HaxJobs centre |
    93	|---|---|---|
    94	| Instructions | Repository rules, coding style, safe edits | Get the user interviews and hired without inventing evidence, job facts, or consent |
    95	| Context delivery | Relevant files, symbols, errors, git diff | Current user request, one career direction, relevant evidence, job source snapshot, company facts, prior decisions |
    96	| Context management | Keep logs and file dumps out of the window | Keep irrelevant career history, stale jobs, unsupported claims, and unrelated tracks out of the run |
    97	| Tools | Read, edit, shell, tests | Inspect a job source, retrieve career evidence, research a company, save a decision, prepare an approved career action |
    98	| Execution environment | Filesystem, subprocess, network, secrets | Personal-data scope, source trust, provider credentials, cost, and approval for sending, contacting, or submitting |
    99	| Durable state | Session, workspace, code changes | Career facts, evidence provenance, job observations, decisions, outcomes, commitments, and conversation continuity |
   100	| Orchestration | Plan edits, run tests, retry commands | Move from lead to verified role to fit judgement to user decision without skipping evidence or approval |
   101	| Sub-agents | Isolated code research or implementation | Focused company research, recruiter research, interview simulation, or parallel source inspection when later justified |
   102	| Skills | Repeatable coding procedure | Repeatable job-search procedure proven useful across real runs |
   103	| Verification | Test, typecheck, artifact receipt | Current source evidence, identity, claim provenance, liveness, saved decision, and exact approval receipt |
   104	| Observability | Model calls, tool calls, code changes, tests | Which user facts and job evidence Hax saw, what it inferred, what it could not verify, actions, cost, and final answer |
   105	| Evolution | Fix recurring coding-agent failure | Turn repeated bad fit calls, missing evidence, noisy updates, or source mistakes into tested harness changes |
   106	
   107	## The biggest job-native differences
   108	
   109	### 1. The person is the long-lived subject
   110	
   111	A coding agent usually starts from the current repository state. Hax starts from a person whose goals, evidence, constraints, and history continue across jobs.
   112	
   113	The harness must select the right slice of that person for the current task. Dumping a giant profile into every prompt would be the career equivalent of dumping an entire repository into Claude Code.
   114	
   115	### 2. Truth has two sides
   116	
   117	Hax compares:
   118	
   119	- claims about the user
   120	- claims about the job or company
   121	
   122	Both sides need sources and dates. A confident overlap between two unsupported claims is still unsupported.
   123	
   124	### 3. Time matters more
   125	
   126	A code file normally remains the same until someone edits it. Jobs close, careers pages change, work authorization changes, user priorities move, and hiring claims become stale.
   127	
   128	Currentness and observation time are part of the evidence, not optional metadata.
   129	
   130	### 4. The final answer can change real life
   131	
   132	A wrong code suggestion may fail a test. A wrong career claim can waste hours, expose private information, damage trust, or cause a dishonest application.
   133	
   134	Hax needs a clear difference between:
   135	
   136	- discussing
   137	- researching
   138	- saving internal state
   139	- drafting an external action
   140	- causing an external effect
   141	
   142	### 5. Success is not one deterministic test command
   143	
   144	A job assessment cannot be proven by `pytest` alone.
   145	
   146	We need several kinds of checking:
   147	
   148	- deterministic checks for source identity, schemas, and saved state
   149	- evidence checks for material claims
   150	- behavioural evaluations for model judgement
   151	- later outcome evidence from replies, interviews, offers, and rejections
   152	
   153	The first experiment needs only the first three.
   154	
   155	# The first harness experiment
   156	
   157	## Goal
   158	
   159	Build the smallest Hax that can answer one real question:
   160	
   161	> "What do you think of this role? Is it worth my time?"
   162	
   163	It receives one frozen career-context fixture and one real job fixture. It must return a conversational answer while staying honest about evidence and uncertainty.
   164	
   165	## Why start here instead of company watching
   166	
   167	Watching adds scheduling, repeated checks, source changes, deduplication, delivery, and durable commitments before we know whether Hax can reason about one job properly.
   168	
   169	The thin job-review task tests the central job-native loop first:
   170	
   171	```text
   172	user question
   173	+ selected career context
   174	+ job evidence
   175	-> model judgement
   176	-> optional source inspection
   177	-> evidence-bearing answer
   178	-> saved trace
   179	```
   180	
   181	Once this works, company watching can repeatedly feed new jobs into the same review capability.
   182	
   183	## Fixture order
   184	
   185	The existing fixture contains five real records with useful defects.
   186	
   187	### First run: Job 49, Trainline IT Support Analyst
   188	
   189	Why first:
   190	
   191	- it has a substantial job description
   192	- the correct broad decision should be easy
   193	- it tests whether Hax follows the user's career direction rather than matching random technical words
   194	- it tests whether Hax refuses to treat unsupported sponsorship data as fact
   195	- it gives us a clean control case before adding web research
   196	
   197	Expected broad behaviour:
   198	
   199	- recognise that this is internal IT support, not backend or AI engineering
   200	- explain the mismatch using actual job responsibilities
   201	- avoid being distracted by mentions of automation, AI, Azure, or Bash
   202	- avoid claiming sponsorship is safe or unsafe without evidence
   203	- tell the user it is probably not worth their time
   204	- stay conversational rather than dumping a scoring report
   205	
   206	### Second run: Job 328, Oritain Software Engineer
   207	
   208	Why second:
   209	
   210	- the title looks promising
   211	- the stored record contains only a title and LinkedIn URL
   212	- the old evaluation confidently invented or imported detail that the stored evidence cannot support
   213	- it forces Hax to recognise insufficient evidence
   214	- it tells us whether source-inspection should become the first real tool
   215	
   216	Expected broad behaviour before a source tool exists:
   217	
   218	- say there is not enough verified job information for a proper fit judgement
   219	- avoid repeating Django, FastAPI, TypeScript, cloud, company mission, or sponsorship claims unless the current run received evidence for them
   220	- identify what needs to be checked next

text:
221	
   222	This contrast is valuable. Job 49 tests judgement with enough job evidence. Job 328 tests honesty when evidence is missing.
   223	
   224	# Minimal build staircase
   225	
   226	We should add one capability at a time and keep the earlier run as a comparison.
   227	
   228	## Stage 0: bare observed model call
   229	
   230	Minimum pieces:
   231	
   232	1. one model-client function
   233	2. one stable Hax instruction block
   234	3. one task-specific instruction block for reviewing a job
   235	4. one current context bundle containing the user request, career fixture, and job fixture
   236	5. one model call
   237	6. one saved run record
   238	7. one human evaluation note
   239	
   240	No tools. No sessions. No compaction. No sub-agents. No skill system. No scheduler. No database.
   241	
   242	This stage tells us what the configured model does when given a clean, source-labelled desk.
   243	
   244	## Stage 1: bounded agent loop with one source tool
   245	
   246	Add only after Job 328 proves the need:
   247	
   248	- tool schema and registry
   249	- one restricted `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` action
   250	- maximum tool-step count
   251	- structured tool result with source, observation time, status, content, and failure
   252	- tool-call trace
   253	
   254	The tool performs normal retrieval code. The model decides whether it needs the source.
   255	
   256	If LinkedIn blocks access or the role is gone, that failure is a valid result. Hax should say the role cannot currently be verified rather than filling the gap with guesses.
   257	
   258	## Stage 2: conversational correction
   259	
   260	Add message history only after testing a follow-up such as:
   261	
   262	> "I actually have three years of Java experience from before university."
   263	
   264	This tests:
   265	
   266	- whether the conversation remains natural
   267	- whether Hax accepts a clear user correction
   268	- whether self-report and evidence strength stay separate
   269	- whether the revised answer uses the correction without rewriting the original job evidence
   270	
   271	Durable cross-session memory can wait. First prove correct handling inside one short conversation.
   272	
   273	## Stage 3: one saved product decision
   274	
   275	Add one narrow internal write after the user says:
   276	
   277	> "Yeah, skip it."
   278	
   279	The result should record the user's decision against the reviewed job and career context.
   280	
   281	This is the first product effect. It should be ordinary typed code, checked after writing. It does not need a general workflow engine.
   282	
   283	# The smallest harness shape
   284	
   285	```text
   286	CLI test runner
   287	    |
   288	    v
   289	Hax run controller
   290	    |-- build stable instructions
   291	    |-- load one fixture bundle
   292	    |-- select allowed tools for this experiment
   293	    |-- call model through one seam
   294	    |-- validate and dispatch requested tools
   295	    |-- stop on final answer or step limit
   296	    |-- save trace and result
   297	    v
   298	Model + narrow normal-code actions
   299	```
   300	
   301	## Minimum modules by responsibility
   302	
   303	These are responsibilities, not final filenames:
   304	
   305	- **model boundary:** one function that sends messages and receives a normalized response
   306	- **instruction builder:** stable Hax rules plus one job-review flow
   307	- **context assembler:** source-labelled career and job material for this run
   308	- **run loop:** message history, tool requests, step budget, final answer
   309	- **tool registry:** absent in Stage 0, one tool in Stage 1
   310	- **trace writer:** records what entered the run and what happened
   311	- **evaluation runner:** applies the fixture checklist and saves human observations
   312	
   313	The interface is a small CLI command or script. The web UI stays out.
   314	
   315	# First instruction hypothesis
   316	
   317	The Stage 0 stable instructions should be short enough to inspect:
   318	
   319	```text
   320	You are Hax, the user's employment agent.
   321	Your goal is to help the user get interviews and become more employable.
   322	Speak naturally and directly.
   323	Never invent user evidence, job facts, company facts, sponsorship, or currentness.
   324	Separate what is supported, what the user stated, what you inferred, and what remains unknown.
   325	Use the supplied career evidence and job evidence only within their stated source and date.
   326	Do not send, submit, contact, or represent the user externally.
   327	```
   328	
   329	The job-review flow adds:
   330	
   331	```text
   332	Decide whether this role deserves the user's time.
   333	Check hard constraints before softer fit.
   334	Explain the strongest real overlap, the main blockers, and important unknowns.
   335	If job evidence is insufficient, say so and identify the next check instead of guessing.
   336	Return a natural answer, not a generic scorecard.
   337	```
   338	
   339	These are hypotheses. We keep, remove, or rewrite them based on the saved runs.
   340	
   341	# First context hypothesis
   342	
   343	Stage 0 should receive four clearly labelled blocks:
   344	
   345	1. user request
   346	2. career direction and constraints
   347	3. relevant user evidence
   348	4. job source snapshot
   349	
   350	Every block should name its source and observation date where applicable.
   351	
   352	Do not include:
   353	
   354	- the whole old database row
   355	- the old model's fit score
   356	- the old evaluation prose
   357	- unrelated career tracks
   358	- every user project
   359	- imagined company research
   360	
   361	The fixture's old evaluation remains hidden from Hax. We use it only as evidence of what went wrong before.
   362	
   363	# What is a tool in the first experiment
   364	
   365	A tool is an action Hax may need during the current run.
   366	
   367	The likely first tool is:
   368	
   369	```text
   370	inspect_job_source(job_ref)
   371	```
   372	
   373	The action resolves the trusted source URL from the supplied fixture or saved job record. The model does not receive arbitrary network-fetch authority.
   374	
   375	It should return:
   376	
   377	- whether retrieval succeeded
   378	- final resolved URL
   379	- observed time
   380	- source type
   381	- current liveness when detectable
   382	- bounded page content or structured job fields
   383	- warnings and failure code
   384	
   385	It must not return a confident fit decision. Retrieval is normal code. Judgement remains with Hax.
   386	
   387	The following are not tools yet:
   388	
   389	- career profile text, because the context assembler supplies the selected slice
   390	- the job fixture, because the current task already supplies it
   391	- a company-watch workflow, because repeated monitoring is not part of the first run
   392	- a skill catalogue, because no repeated successful procedure has been observed
   393	- sub-agent spawning, because one job review is not context-heavy enough
   394	
   395	# Commands, workflows, and skills
   396	
   397	We should discover these from usage instead of naming a large catalogue now.
   398	
   399	## Command
   400	
   401	A command is how a person or test starts a known action.
   402	
   403	First candidate:
   404	
   405	```text
   406	haxjobs experiment review-job --fixture job-49
   407	```
   408	
   409	The exact syntax can wait until code inspection. The requirement is one repeatable way to run the same fixture with the same inputs.
   410	
   411	## Workflow
   412	
   413	A workflow is controller-owned order that must not be skipped.
   414	
   415	Stage 0 needs almost none:
   416	
   417	```text
   418	load fixture -> build context -> call model -> save trace -> stop
   419	```
   420	
   421	Stage 1 adds bounded tool dispatch. Company monitoring remains later.
   422	
   423	## Skill
   424	
   425	A skill is a saved procedure learned from repeated successful work.
   426	
   427	We should not write a `review-job` skill before seeing whether the stable instructions and flow prompt are enough. If repeated runs show the model consistently misses the same ordered method, that method may later become a skill.
   428	
   429	The promotion rule is:
   430	
   431	```text
   432	one successful prompt trick: keep it in the experiment
   433	repeated stable procedure: consider a skill
   434	mandatory safe ordering: put it in normal workflow code
   435	one narrow action: make it a tool
   436	```
   437	
   438	# Observability for the first run
   439	
   440	We cannot refine what we cannot inspect.

   284	
   285	```text
   286	CLI test runner
   287	    |
   288	    v
   289	Hax run controller
   290	    |-- build stable instructions
   291	    |-- load one fixture bundle
   292	    |-- select allowed tools for this experiment
   293	    |-- call model through one seam
   294	    |-- validate and dispatch requested tools
   295	    |-- stop on final answer or step limit
   296	    |-- save trace and result
   297	    v
   298	Model + narrow normal-code actions
   299	```
   300	
   301	## Minimum modules by responsibility
   302	
   303	These are responsibilities, not final filenames:
   304	
   305	- **model boundary:** one function that sends messages and receives a normalized response
   306	- **instruction builder:** stable Hax rules plus one job-review flow
   307	- **context assembler:** source-labelled career and job material for this run
   308	- **run loop:** message history, tool requests, step budget, final answer
   309	- **tool registry:** absent in Stage 0, one tool in Stage 1
   310	- **trace writer:** records what entered the run and what happened
   311	- **evaluation runner:** applies the fixture checklist and saves human observations
   312	
   313	The interface is a small CLI command or script. The web UI stays out.
   314	
   315	# First instruction hypothesis
   316	
   317	The Stage 0 stable instructions should be short enough to inspect:
   318	
   319	```text
   320	You are Hax, the user's employment agent.
   321	Your goal is to help the user get interviews and become more employable.
   322	Speak naturally and directly.
   323	Never invent user evidence, job facts, company facts, sponsorship, or currentness.
   324	Separate what is supported, what the user stated, what you inferred, and what remains unknown.
   325	Use the supplied career evidence and job evidence only within their stated source and date.
   326	Do not send, submit, contact, or represent the user externally.
   327	```
   328	
   329	The job-review flow adds:
   330	
   331	```text
   332	Decide whether this role deserves the user's time.
   333	Check hard constraints before softer fit.
   334	Explain the strongest real overlap, the main blockers, and important unknowns.
   335	If job evidence is insufficient, say so and identify the next check instead of guessing.
   336	Return a natural answer, not a generic scorecard.
   337	```
   338	
   339	These are hypotheses. We keep, remove, or rewrite them based on the saved runs.
   340	
   341	# First context hypothesis
   342	
   343	Stage 0 should receive four clearly labelled blocks:
   344	
   345	1. user request
   346	2. career direction and constraints
   347	3. relevant user evidence
   348	4. job source snapshot
   349	
   350	Every block should name its source and observation date where applicable.
   351	
   352	Do not include:
   353	
   354	- the whole old database row
   355	- the old model's fit score
   356	- the old evaluation prose
   357	- unrelated career tracks
   358	- every user project
   359	- imagined company research
   360	
   361	The fixture's old evaluation remains hidden from Hax. We use it only as evidence of what went wrong before.
   362	
   363	# What is a tool in the first experiment
   364	
   365	A tool is an action Hax may need during the current run.
   366	
   367	The likely first tool is:
   368	
   369	```text
   370	inspect_job_source(job_ref)
   371	```
   372	
   373	The action resolves the trusted source URL from the supplied fixture or saved job record. The model does not receive arbitrary network-fetch authority.
   374	
   375	It should return:
   376	
   377	- whether retrieval succeeded
   378	- final resolved URL
   379	- observed time
   380	- source type
   381	- current liveness when detectable
   382	- bounded page content or structured job fields
   383	- warnings and failure code
   384	
   385	It must not return a confident fit decision. Retrieval is normal code. Judgement remains with Hax.
   386	
   387	The following are not tools yet:
   388	
   389	- career profile text, because the context assembler supplies the selected slice
   390	- the job fixture, because the current task already supplies it
   391	- a company-watch workflow, because repeated monitoring is not part of the first run
   392	- a skill catalogue, because no repeated successful procedure has been observed
   393	- sub-agent spawning, because one job review is not context-heavy enough
   394	
   395	# Commands, workflows, and skills
   396	
   397	We should discover these from usage instead of naming a large catalogue now.
   398	
   399	## Command
   400	
   401	A command is how a person or test starts a known action.
   402	
   403	First candidate:
   404	
   405	```text
   406	haxjobs experiment review-job --fixture job-49
   407	```
   408	
   409	The exact syntax can wait until code inspection. The requirement is one repeatable way to run the same fixture with the same inputs.
   410	
   411	## Workflow
   412	
   413	A workflow is controller-owned order that must not be skipped.
   414	
   415	Stage 0 needs almost none:
   416	
   417	```text
   418	load fixture -> build context -> call model -> save trace -> stop
   419	```
   420	
   421	Stage 1 adds bounded tool dispatch. Company monitoring remains later.
   422	
   423	## Skill
   424	
   425	A skill is a saved procedure learned from repeated successful work.
   426	
   427	We should not write a `review-job` skill before seeing whether the stable instructions and flow prompt are enough. If repeated runs show the model consistently misses the same ordered method, that method may later become a skill.
   428	
   429	The promotion rule is:
   430	
   431	```text
   432	one successful prompt trick: keep it in the experiment
   433	repeated stable procedure: consider a skill
   434	mandatory safe ordering: put it in normal workflow code
   435	one narrow action: make it a tool
   436	```
   437	
   438	# Observability for the first run
   439	
   440	We cannot refine what we cannot inspect.

text:
441	
   442	Every run should save:
   443	
   444	- run ID and timestamp
   445	- model and model settings
   446	- instruction version
   447	- fixture IDs and hashes
   448	- exact context manifest
   449	- messages sent to and returned from the model
   450	- tool requests and results when Stage 1 exists
   451	- token usage and duration when available
   452	- final answer
   453	- errors and stop reason
   454	- evaluator notes
   455	
   456	Raw private content should stay local. The first build does not need telemetry infrastructure. A local JSON run record plus a Markdown review is enough.
   457	
   458	# Evaluation checklist
   459	
   460	The first evaluation is a human-readable checklist, not a second model judging the first model.
   461	
   462	## Job 49 checks
   463	
   464	- Did Hax identify the role as IT support rather than backend engineering?
   465	- Did it use concrete responsibilities from the supplied job source?
   466	- Did it avoid overvaluing stray words such as AI, automation, Azure, and Bash?
   467	- Did it keep sponsorship unknown?
   468	- Did it make a clear time-worthiness recommendation?
   469	- Did it sound like Hax rather than an ATS report?
   470	- Did every material claim come from supplied context?
   471	
   472	## Job 328 checks
   473	
   474	- Did Hax notice that the job evidence is only a title and URL?
   475	- Did it avoid repeating claims from the hidden old evaluation?
   476	- Did it refuse a confident fit decision without enough job evidence?
   477	- Did it name the next useful check?
   478	- After Stage 1, did it use the source tool only when needed?
   479	- If retrieval failed, did it preserve uncertainty rather than guessing?
   480	
   481	## Engineering checks
   482	
   483	- Can the model boundary be replaced with a fake?
   484	- Does the same fixture produce a complete saved trace?
   485	- Is context source-labelled and bounded?
   486	- Does the run stop at its step limit?
   487	- Are unknown tool calls and malformed arguments recorded as failures?
   488	- Can the run be repeated without manually editing code?
   489	
   490	# What we explicitly do not build yet
   491	
   492	- company monitoring
   493	- schedulers or workers
   494	- full career graph
   495	- long-term retrieval
   496	- compaction
   497	- embeddings
   498	- sub-agents
   499	- skill loading
   500	- application packs
   501	- outreach
   502	- application submission
   503	- browser UI
   504	- multi-provider fallback
   505	- automatic self-improvement
   506	- generalized workflow engine
   507	- final database schema
   508	
   509	These are not rejected. They need a failure or a proven next task before entering the minimal harness.
   510	
   511	# How refinement should work
   512	
   513	After each run, write four things:
   514	
   515	1. what Hax received
   516	2. what Hax did
   517	3. what was wrong or surprisingly good
   518	4. the smallest harness change that could explainably improve it
   519	
   520	Then rerun the same fixture.
   521	
   522	Examples:
   523	
   524	| Observed failure | Likely harness change |
   525	|---|---|
   526	| Invents job requirements | Tighten source labels or evidence instruction; add claim check |
   527	| Misses a relevant project | Improve context selection, not the model prompt first |
   528	| Calls source tool unnecessarily | Improve tool description or tool availability for the flow |
   529	| Repeats unsupported sponsorship claim | Add scoped evidence rules and a regression case |
   530	| Becomes confused after follow-up | Fix history assembly before adding durable memory |
   531	| Produces a good method repeatedly | Consider promoting the method into a skill |
   532	| Skips a mandatory safety step | Move that step into controller code, not another reminder |
   533	
   534	This is the HaxJobs version of the evolution primitive. The change follows a repeatable failure and keeps a regression case.
   535	
   536	# Current recommendations
   537	
   538	1. Freeze one truthful backend career-context fixture before code.
   539	2. Run Job 49 first as the clear-evidence control case.
   540	3. Run Job 328 second as the missing-evidence stress case.
   541	4. Start Stage 0 with no tools and save the exact run.
   542	5. Add only `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` for Stage 1.
   543	6. Use the configured real model, with no provider fallback.
   544	7. Save local run artifacts and human review notes.
   545	8. Keep the web app, company watching, database redesign, sessions, compaction, skills, and sub-agents out of the first build.
   546	
   547	# Questions that need Arinze's judgement
   548	
   549	1. **Fixture order:** Decided. Run Job 49 first as the clear-evidence control case, then Job 328 as the case that should force source inspection.
   550	2. **Career fixture:** Decided. Create one frozen backend-career fixture from the user's real current profile and evidence, with private source references kept local.
   551	3. **Build staircase:** Decided. Stage 0 deliberately has no tools. Stage 1 adds only `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` so the effect of the tool is visible.
   552	
   553	# Decision ledger
   554	
   555	| Item | Status | Current direction |
   556	|---|---|---|
   557	| Active design method | Corrected | Build the smallest Hax harness, run real fixtures, observe, and refine one primitive at a time. |
   558	| Previous company-watch modelling | Paused | Keep accepted behaviour as input, but do not treat its proposed domain model as the architecture. |
   559	| Shared harness spine | Working recommendation | One interface-independent run controller around one model boundary. |
   560	| Job-native difference | Working recommendation | Career context, evidence, source freshness, decisions, outcomes, and approval-bound external effects shape each primitive. |
   561	| First task | Working recommendation | Conversational review of one real job against one frozen career context. |
   562	| First fixture | Decided | Job 49 first, then Job 328. |
   563	| First tool | Decided | None in Stage 0, then only `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` in Stage 1. |
   564	| Career fixture | Decided | A frozen slice of the user's real backend track and evidence. |
   565	| Persistence | Working recommendation | Local run trace and Markdown evaluation only for the first experiment. |
   566	| Skills and sub-agents | Deferred | Add only after repeated work proves a stable procedure or isolation need. |
   567	| Database and UI | Deferred | Not needed to learn from the first runs. |
   568	
   569	# Next step
   570	
   571	Decide the language, small framework set, observability floor, and verification floor for the experiment. Then inspect only the current model boundary and configuration needed for Stage 0.

text:
1	# Five-job development sample for the company-watch slice
     2	
     3	## Why this file exists
     4	
     5	This is the fixed five-job sample selected randomly from `state/haxjobs.db` on 17 July 2026. It gives the company-watch vertical slice real data to walk through before the new system is designed or built.
     6	
     7	The selected job IDs are:
     8	
     9	- `49`
    10	- `203`
    11	- `312`
    12	- `328`
    13	- `374`
    14	
    15	The old database is development evidence only. Its tables, classifications, scores, and mistakes do not define the greenfield data model.
    16	
    17	## What is preserved here
    18	
    19	For each job this file records:
    20	
    21	- every field from the current `jobs` row
    22	- the complete job-description content currently stored for that job
    23	- the job-specific fields from the linked `evaluations` row
    24	- whether decisions, packs, outreach contacts, or outreach drafts exist
    25	- obvious data-quality problems that the new slice must handle
    26	
    27	The `profile_snapshot_json` value is `{}` for all five evaluations.
    28	
    29	Each evaluation also points to the same 170,691-character `report_markdown` value for cycle `test-run-2026-06-29`. That value is a cycle-wide report covering 376 jobs, not a report about the individual job. It is not duplicated five times here. Its presence and size are recorded because this is itself a data-model problem.
    30	
    31	## Sample summary
    32	
    33	| Job ID | Stored title | Stored company | Source | Fit | Stored description |
    34	|---:|---|---|---|---:|---|
    35	| 49 | IT Support Analyst | trainline | Ashby API | 12, Skip | 5,000 characters, cut off mid-sentence |
    36	| 203 | Applied ML Researcher, Fully Remote, up to $90/hr | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 38, Weak fit | 156-character title and URL stub |
    37	| 312 | Associate Data Platform Engineer, Cyber Data Platform | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 52, Good fit | 166-character title and URL stub |
    38	| 328 | Software Engineer, Mid-Level, Full Stack | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 68, Good fit | 149-character title and URL stub |
    39	| 374 | Java Full Stack Developer | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 42, Weak fit | 114-character title and URL stub |
    40	
    41	# Job 49: Trainline IT Support Analyst
    42	
    43	## Stored job row
    44	
    45	| Field | Stored value |
    46	|---|---|
    47	| `id` | `49` |
    48	| `external_id` | `20260607-174539_ashby_api_trainline_IT_Support_Analyst.json` |
    49	| `title` | `IT Support Analyst` |
    50	| `company` | `trainline` |
    51	| `location` | `London` |
    52	| `source_url` | [Ashby application page](https://jobs.ashbyhq.com/trainline/f240bf55-4683-4284-8222-e7cd0c227d70/application) |
    53	| `source` | `ashby_api` |
    54	| `source_quality` | `direct` |
    55	| `status` | `skipped` |
    56	| `discovered_at` | `2026-06-07 20:50:29` |
    57	| `updated_at` | `2026-06-11 18:25:39` |
    58	| `role_family` | `platform_backend` |
    59	| `role_family_confidence` | `0.30` |
    60	| `recommended_cv_variant` | `platform_backend` |
    61	| `role_family_terms` | `platform`, `cloud`, `azure` |
    62	| `classified_at` | `2026-06-11 18:25:39` |
    63	| `pack_status` | `none` |
    64	| `pack_review_status` | `none` |
    65	| `pack_review_notes` | empty |
    66	| `pack_reviewed_at` | null |
    67	| `pack_dir` | empty |
    68	| `outreach_status` | `none` |
    69	
    70	## Complete stored description content
    71	
    72	The database stores exactly 5,000 characters and cuts off during the final candidate section. The content contains:
    73	
    74	### Company context
    75	
    76	- Trainline describes itself as a rail and coach travel platform operating through its mobile app, website, and business partners.
    77	- The listing claims more than 135 million monthly visits, £6.3 billion in annual ticket sales, more than 270 rail and coach partners, operations across more than 40 countries, and a team of more than 1,000 people.
    78	- Named office locations include London, Paris, Barcelona, Milan, Edinburgh, and Madrid.
    79	
    80	### Team and role
    81	
    82	- The role sits in Workplace Technology and the Tech Support team.
    83	- It is a Tier 2 internal IT-support role, not a software-engineering role.
    84	- The estate includes managed MacOS devices, Windows devices, Azure Active Directory, Windows Server, meeting-room equipment, and internal event support.
    85	
    86	### Stored responsibilities
    87	
    88	- Manage the ticket lifecycle, dashboards, workloads, and reporting.
    89	- Resolve incidents within service-level targets and escalate to Tier 3 teams.
    90	- Troubleshoot issues and perform root-cause analysis.
    91	- Progress service requests and support internal employees through the IT-service platform, collaboration tools, and an onsite Tech Bar.
    92	- Manage IT assets, hardware refreshes, inventory, secure disposal, onboarding, offboarding, laptop builds, access changes, and asset retrieval.
    93	- Support meeting-room audio and video equipment, remote administration, internal events, security checks, audits, technical documentation, and knowledge sharing.
    94	- Explore automation, AI, and ML for internal process improvements.
    95	
    96	### Stored technical requirements
    97	
    98	- Windows 11, MacOS, Android, and Linux familiarity.
    99	- JAMF, Intune, mobile-device management, Microsoft 365, Slack, and Atlassian products.
   100	- Active Directory, Azure Active Directory, SaaS systems, and cloud-migration knowledge.
   101	- PCI-DSS, ISO 20071 as written in the source, IT governance, TCP/IP, DNS, DHCP, LAN, WAN, and wireless networking.
   102	- Basic PowerShell, Windows command line, Apple scripting, Bash, security tools, IT-service systems, video-conferencing equipment, Autopilot, MFA, SSO, SAML, and cloud printing.
   103	- Desirable certifications include Microsoft Endpoint Administrator Associate, Apple or JAMF certificates, and CompTIA A+.
   104	
   105	### Truncation
   106	
   107	The stored description ends during the phrase `resolve issues`. Any content after that point was not kept by the old ingestion path.
   108	
   109	## Stored evaluation
   110	
   111	| Field | Stored value |
   112	|---|---|
   113	| `evaluation_id` | `52` |
   114	| `fit_score` | `12` |
   115	| `fit_verdict` | `SKIP` |
   116	| `level` | `4` |
   117	| `level_name` | `Skip` |
   118	| `sponsorship_risk` | `low` |
   119	| `evaluation_decision` | `skipped` |
   120	| `evaluated_by` | `hermes` |
   121	| `evaluated_at` | `2026-06-08 11:00:41` |
   122	| `role_type` | empty |
   123	| `agent` | empty |
   124	| `profile_snapshot_json` | `{}` |
   125	| `report_markdown` | 170,691-character cycle-wide report, omitted |
   126	| `report_cycle_id` | `test-run-2026-06-29` |
   127	| `evaluation_pack_dir` | empty |
   128	| `pack_template_id` | empty |
   129	
   130	Strongest matches recorded:
   131	
   132	- London matches the user's location preference.
   133	- Trainline is a known technology company.
   134	
   135	Major gaps recorded:
   136	
   137	- This is IT support, not software engineering.
   138	- JAMF, Intune, Windows administration, meeting-room support, and ticket work do not match the Python, FastAPI, and AI track.
   139	- The role has no meaningful coding, system-design, or AI-development work.
   140	
   141	Stored summary: this is an enterprise IT-operations role with almost no overlap with the user's Python backend and AI-engineering direction.
   142	
   143	Stored skip reason: the role type and technical work do not match the target career direction. The single automation and AI bullet is a process-improvement detail, not the role's main work.
   144	
   145	## Related state
   146	
   147	- Decisions: `0`
   148	- Packs: none
   149	- Outreach contacts: `0`
   150	- Outreach drafts: `0`
   151	
   152	## Data-quality observations
   153

1	# Five-job development sample for the company-watch slice
     2	
     3	## Why this file exists
     4	
     5	This is the fixed five-job sample selected randomly from `state/haxjobs.db` on 17 July 2026. It gives the company-watch vertical slice real data to walk through before the new system is designed or built.
     6	
     7	The selected job IDs are:
     8	
     9	- `49`
    10	- `203`
    11	- `312`
    12	- `328`
    13	- `374`
    14	
    15	The old database is development evidence only. Its tables, classifications, scores, and mistakes do not define the greenfield data model.
    16	
    17	## What is preserved here
    18	
    19	For each job this file records:
    20	
    21	- every field from the current `jobs` row
    22	- the complete job-description content currently stored for that job
    23	- the job-specific fields from the linked `evaluations` row
    24	- whether decisions, packs, outreach contacts, or outreach drafts exist
    25	- obvious data-quality problems that the new slice must handle
    26	
    27	The `profile_snapshot_json` value is `{}` for all five evaluations.
    28	
    29	Each evaluation also points to the same 170,691-character `report_markdown` value for cycle `test-run-2026-06-29`. That value is a cycle-wide report covering 376 jobs, not a report about the individual job. It is not duplicated five times here. Its presence and size are recorded because this is itself a data-model problem.
    30	
    31	## Sample summary
    32	
    33	| Job ID | Stored title | Stored company | Source | Fit | Stored description |
    34	|---:|---|---|---|---:|---|
    35	| 49 | IT Support Analyst | trainline | Ashby API | 12, Skip | 5,000 characters, cut off mid-sentence |
    36	| 203 | Applied ML Researcher, Fully Remote, up to $90/hr | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 38, Weak fit | 156-character title and URL stub |
    37	| 312 | Associate Data Platform Engineer, Cyber Data Platform | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 52, Good fit | 166-character title and URL stub |
    38	| 328 | Software Engineer, Mid-Level, Full Stack | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 68, Good fit | 149-character title and URL stub |
    39	| 374 | Java Full Stack Developer | LinkedIn | LinkedIn local | 42, Weak fit | 114-character title and URL stub |
    40	
    41	# Job 49: Trainline IT Support Analyst
    42	
    43	## Stored job row
    44	
    45	| Field | Stored value |
    46	|---|---|

    51	| `location` | `London` |

    53	| `source` | `ashby_api` |

    55	| `status` | `skipped` |

    68	| `outreach_status` | `none` |
    69	
    70	## Complete stored description content
    71	
    72	The database stores exactly 5,000 characters and cuts off during the final candidate section. The content contains:
    73	
    74	### Company context
    75	
    76	- Trainline describes itself as a rail and coach travel platform operating through its mobile app, website, and business partners.
    77	- The listing claims more than 135 million monthly visits, £6.3 billion in annual ticket sales, more than 270 rail and coach partners, operations across more than 40 countries, and a team of more than 1,000 people.
    78	- Named office locations include London, Paris, Barcelona, Milan, Edinburgh, and Madrid.
    79	
    80	### Team and role
    81	
    82	- The role sits in Workplace Technology and the Tech Support team.
    83	- It is a Tier 2 internal IT-support role, not a software-engineering role.
    84	- The estate includes managed MacOS devices, Windows devices, Azure Active Directory, Windows Server, meeting-room equipment, and internal event support.
    85	
    86	### Stored responsibilities
    87	
    88	- Manage the ticket lifecycle, dashboards, workloads, and reporting.
    89	- Resolve incidents within service-level targets and escalate to Tier 3 teams.
    90	- Troubleshoot issues and perform root-cause analysis.
    91	- Progress service requests and support internal employees through the IT-service platform, collaboration tools, and an onsite Tech Bar.
    92	- Manage IT assets, hardware refreshes, inventory, secure disposal, onboarding, offboarding, laptop builds, access changes, and asset retrieval.
    93	- Support meeting-room audio and video equipment, remote administration, internal events, security checks, audits, technical documentation, and knowledge sharing.
    94	- Explore automation, AI, and ML for internal process improvements.
    95	
    96	### Stored technical requirements
    97	
    98	- Windows 11, MacOS, Android, and Linux familiarity.
    99	- JAMF, Intune, mobile-device management, Microsoft 365, Slack, and Atlassian products.
   100	- Active Directory, Azure Active Directory, SaaS systems, and cloud-migration knowledge.
   101	- PCI-DSS, ISO 20071 as written in the source, IT governance, TCP/IP, DNS, DHCP, LAN, WAN, and wireless networking.
   102	- Basic PowerShell, Windows command line, Apple scripting, Bash, security tools, IT-service systems, video-conferencing equipment, Autopilot, MFA, SSO, SAML, and cloud printing.
   103	- Desirable certifications include Microsoft Endpoint Administrator Associate, Apple or JAMF certificates, and CompTIA A+.
   104	
   105	### Truncation
   106	
   107	The stored description ends during the phrase `resolve issues`. Any content after that point was not kept by the old ingestion path.
   108	
   109	## Stored evaluation
   110	
   111	| Field | Stored value |
   112	|---|---|
   113	| `evaluation_id` | `52` |
   114	| `fit_score` | `12` |
   115	| `fit_verdict` | `SKIP` |

   123	| `agent` | empty |
   124	| `profile_snapshot_json` | `{}` |
   125	| `report_markdown` | 170,691-character cycle-wide report, omitted |
   126	| `report_cycle_id` | `test-run-2026-06-29` |
   127	| `evaluation_pack_dir` | empty |
   128	| `pack_template_id` | empty |
   129	
   130	Strongest matches recorded:
   131	
   132	- London matches the user's location preference.
   133	- Trainline is a known technology company.
   134	
   135	Major gaps recorded:
   136	
   137	- This is IT support, not software engineering.
   138	- JAMF, Intune, Windows administration, meeting-room support, and ticket work do not match the Python, FastAPI, and AI track.
   139	- The role has no meaningful coding, system-design, or AI-development work.
   140	
   141	Stored summary: this is an enterprise IT-operations role with almost no overlap with the user's Python backend and AI-engineering direction.
   142	
   143	Stored skip reason: the role type and technical work do not match the target career direction. The single automation and AI bullet is a process-improvement detail, not the role's main work.
   144	
   145	## Related state

   147	- Decisions: `0`

text:
1	---
     2	status: decided
     3	created: 2026-07-17
     4	scope: Choose the language, framework floor, observability floor, and verification floor for the first Hax experiment
     5	builds_on:
     6	  - discussion/004-minimal-job-native-harness.md
     7	research_date: 2026-07-17
     8	---
     9	
    10	# Implementation stack, observability, and verification
    11	
    12	## Decisions carried forward
    13	
    14	The first harness experiment is now decided:
    15	
    16	- freeze one truthful backend-career fixture from the user's real evidence
    17	- run Job 49 first as the clear-evidence control
    18	- run Job 328 second as the missing-evidence stress case
    19	- Stage 0 has no tools
    20	- Stage 1 adds only `inspect_job_source(job_ref)`
    21	
    22	Before code, we need to choose enough of the implementation stack to make those experiments useful and repeatable.
    23	
    24	We do not need the final cloud architecture. We need a good first foundation that does not trap HaxJobs later.
    25	
    26	# Short recommendation
    27	
    28	Use:
    29	
    30	```text
    31	Python for Hax and all career product logic
    32	Pydantic for model-facing and trust-boundary contracts
    33	plain Python for the first run loop
    34	one real provider adapter plus one fake adapter
    35	pytest for deterministic checks
    36	local redacted JSONL for agent traces
    37	Python logging for normal diagnostics
    38	Markdown rubrics for human review
    39	uv for environment and dependency locking
    40	TypeScript later for the web interface only
    41	```
    42	
    43	Do not add an agent framework, telemetry server, workflow engine, eval platform, Go service, or Rust service for Stage 0.
    44	
    45	The first framework we should reconsider is PydanticAI. The first trace UI we should reconsider is Phoenix. The first durable workflow system we should reconsider is Temporal. Each has a concrete activation condition below.
    46	
    47	# First, observability and verification are different
    48	
    49	These terms get mixed together a lot.
    50	
    51	## Observability
    52	
    53	Observability answers:
    54	
    55	> What happened inside this run?
    56	
    57	For example:
    58	
    59	- which instructions Hax received
    60	- which career and job fixtures were selected
    61	- which model was called
    62	- how long it took
    63	- what the model returned
    64	- which tools it requested
    65	- what each tool returned
    66	- why the run stopped
    67	- token usage and provider errors
    68	
    69	It is the flight recorder.
    70	
    71	## Verification
    72	
    73	Verification answers:
    74	
    75	> Was what happened acceptable and useful?
    76	
    77	For example:
    78	
    79	- did Hax identify Job 49 as IT support?
    80	- did it keep sponsorship unknown?
    81	- did it invent details for Job 328?
    82	- did a fake model's malformed tool arguments get rejected?
    83	- did the run stop at the step limit?
    84	- did Hax make a clear recommendation?
    85	
    86	It is the inspection after the flight.
    87	
    88	We need both from the first experiment, but neither requires a platform.
    89	
    90	# Language choice
    91	
    92	## Recommendation: Python
    93	
    94	Python gives HaxJobs the best combined fit for:
    95	
    96	- model provider SDKs
    97	- typed model and tool contracts through Pydantic
    98	- evaluation libraries
    99	- HTTP, HTML, PDF, data, and browser work
   100	- SQLite in the standard library
   101	- CLI development
   102	- FastAPI later
   103	- the user's existing experience
   104	- fast iteration while the product is still being discovered
   105	
   106	The harness mostly waits on model calls, web pages, browsers, and storage. Rust or Go speed would not make those remote systems answer faster.
   107	
   108	## Comparison
   109	
   110	| Question | Python | TypeScript | Go | Rust |
   111	|---|---|---|---|---|
   112	| Major model SDK support | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Thin, often REST or community clients |
   113	| Agent and evaluation libraries | Strongest current choice | Strong and improving | Limited | Limited |
   114	| Runtime validation for model data | Pydantic is a very good fit | Zod is a very good fit | More manual glue | Strong types, more schema plumbing |
   115	| Static web and document processing | Excellent | Good | Good | More work |
   116	| Browser automation | Official Playwright support | Best native Playwright experience | Community binding | Community binding |
   117	| Async network work | Good enough | Very good | Excellent | Excellent |
   118	| CPU and memory performance | Weakest | Fine | Strong | Strongest |
   119	| Native single-binary packaging | Awkward | Improving | Excellent | Excellent |
   120	| Speed for one developer building this product | Best fit | Close second | Slower | Slowest |
   121	| Later React interface | Separate API boundary | Same language | Separate API boundary | Separate API boundary |
   122	
   123	## Why TypeScript is the real alternative
   124	
   125	TypeScript has a serious case:
   126	
   127	- the web interface is already React and TypeScript
   128	- Playwright starts in the Node world
   129	- Zod gives good runtime validation
   130	- OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini support TypeScript well
   131	- one language can reduce mental switching
   132	
   133	But the one-language benefit is smaller than it looks. The browser interface and agent runtime still have different trust boundaries, dependencies, release cycles, and data access. We need runtime validation across that boundary anyway.
   134	
   135	HaxJobs' centre is model work, research, document processing, evaluation, and career data. Python wins that combination.
   136	
   137	Use TypeScript later as a thin interface over the same Python actions. Do not rewrite career logic in the browser or Node server.
   138	
   139	## Why not Go now
   140	
   141	Go would give us:
   142	
   143	- easy native binaries
   144	- simple deployment
   145	- cheap concurrency
   146	- predictable resource usage
   147	
   148	It loses on:
   149	
   150	- agent and evaluation library depth
   151	- research and document tooling
   152	- model-specific experimentation speed
   153	- the amount of glue needed around structured outputs and tests
   154	
   155	Go becomes interesting if discovery later needs thousands of concurrent fetches and profiling shows Python is the actual limit. That would justify a small fetch worker, not a rewrite of Hax.
   156	
   157	## Why not Rust now
   158	
   159	Rust would give us:
   160	
   161	- strong memory and concurrency checks
   162	- fast, small native programs
   163	- good control over resources
   164	
   165	It would also make every experiment slower to write and change. The main HaxJobs risks are bad permissions, false claims, private-data leakage, prompt injection, stale sources, and external actions without approval. Rust does not solve those.
   166	
   167	Rust becomes interesting for a constrained native component, untrusted local execution, or a hard resource target. None of those exists in Stage 0.
   168	
   169	# Exact Python floor
   170	
   171	## Python version
   172	
   173	Use a currently supported Python version that all selected dependencies support. Python 3.12 is the conservative floor. Python 3.13 is fine if the live dependency check is clean.
   174	
   175	Do not make a language-version decision from fashion. Pin the version after the Stage 0 dependency check.
   176	
   177	## Environment and packaging
   178	
   179	Use `uv` because the repository already uses it and it handles:
   180	
   181	- Python versions
   182	- virtual environments
   183	- locked dependencies
   184	- running tools
   185	- package installation
   186	
   187	No Poetry, Conda, pip-tools, or second package manager.
   188	
   189	## CLI
   190	
   191	Use the standard library's `argparse` for the experiment runner unless the command shape becomes painful.
   192	
   193	One command does not justify Typer or Click. Reconsider Typer when the real CLI has enough nested product commands that help text and typed option handling remove meaningful code.
   194	
   195	# Framework choice
   196	
   197	## Recommendation: plain Python plus Pydantic contracts
   198	
   199	The first loop is small:
   200	
   201	```python
   202	messages = build_context(fixture)
   203	response = model.complete(messages)
   204	save_trace(response)
   205	return response.text
   206	```
   207	
   208	Stage 1 adds a bounded loop:
   209	
   210	```python
   211	for step in range(max_steps):
   212	    response = model.complete(messages, tools=allowed_tools)
   213	    record_model_response(response)
   214	
   215	    if not response.tool_calls:
   216	        return response.text
   217	
   218	    messages.append(response.as_assistant_message())
   219	    for call in response.tool_calls:
   220	        result = dispatch(call)
   221	        messages.append(result.as_tool_message())
   222	```
   223	
   224	That does not need LangChain, LangGraph, PydanticAI, or the OpenAI Agents SDK yet.
   225	
   226	Pydantic should validate:
   227	
   228	- normalized model responses
   229	- tool arguments
   230	- tool results
   231	- fixture records
   232	- trace events
   233	- later product actions at trust boundaries
   234	
   235	Pydantic is a contract library here. It is not the architecture.
   236	
   237	## Why not PydanticAI from the first line
   238	
   239	PydanticAI is the strongest framework candidate for HaxJobs. It already has:
   240	
   241	- typed dependencies and outputs
   242	- broad model support
   243	- message history
   244	- toolsets
   245	- deferred tools and approvals
   246	- fake and function models for tests
   247	- OpenTelemetry instrumentation
   248	- multi-agent patterns
   249	- Temporal integration
   250	
   251	That is exactly why we should keep it nearby.
   252	
   253	But Stage 0 is supposed to expose the real primitive. If we begin inside a framework, we may learn the framework's message types and lifecycle before we learn what HaxJobs itself needs.
   254	
   255	Start plain. Switch when PydanticAI can delete tested plumbing rather than merely replacing ten obvious lines with its own vocabulary.
   256	
   257	### PydanticAI activation condition
   258	
   259	Reconsider it when the local loop has accumulated several of these:
   260
