1	---
     2	status: decided
     3	created: 2026-07-17
     4	scope: Choose the language, framework floor, observability floor, and verification floor for the first Hax experiment
     5	builds_on:
     6	  - discussion/004-minimal-job-native-harness.md
     7	research_date: 2026-07-17
     8	---
     9	
    10	# Implementation stack, observability, and verification
    11	
    12	## Decisions carried forward
    13	
    14	The first harness experiment is now decided:
    15	
    16	- freeze one truthful backend-career fixture from the user's real evidence
    17	- run Job 49 first as the clear-evidence control
    18	- run Job 328 second as the missing-evidence stress case
    19	- Stage 0 has no tools
    20	- Stage 1 adds only `inspect_job_source(job_ref)`
    21	
    22	Before code, we need to choose enough of the implementation stack to make those experiments useful and repeatable.
    23	
    24	We do not need the final cloud architecture. We need a good first foundation that does not trap HaxJobs later.
    25	
    26	# Short recommendation
    27	
    28	Use:
    29	
    30	```text
    31	Python for Hax and all career product logic
    32	Pydantic for model-facing and trust-boundary contracts
    33	plain Python for the first run loop
    34	one real provider adapter plus one fake adapter
    35	pytest for deterministic checks
    36	local redacted JSONL for agent traces
    37	Python logging for normal diagnostics
    38	Markdown rubrics for human review
    39	uv for environment and dependency locking
    40	TypeScript later for the web interface only
    41	```
    42	
    43	Do not add an agent framework, telemetry server, workflow engine, eval platform, Go service, or Rust service for Stage 0.
    44	
    45	The first framework we should reconsider is PydanticAI. The first trace UI we should reconsider is Phoenix. The first durable workflow system we should reconsider is Temporal. Each has a concrete activation condition below.
    46	
    47	# First, observability and verification are different
    48	
    49	These terms get mixed together a lot.
    50	
    51	## Observability
    52	
    53	Observability answers:
    54	
    55	> What happened inside this run?
    56	
    57	For example:
    58	
    59	- which instructions Hax received
    60	- which career and job fixtures were selected
    61	- which model was called
    62	- how long it took
    63	- what the model returned
    64	- which tools it requested
    65	- what each tool returned
    66	- why the run stopped
    67	- token usage and provider errors
    68	
    69	It is the flight recorder.
    70	
    71	## Verification
    72	
    73	Verification answers:
    74	
    75	> Was what happened acceptable and useful?
    76	
    77	For example:
    78	
    79	- did Hax identify Job 49 as IT support?
    80	- did it keep sponsorship unknown?
    81	- did it invent details for Job 328?
    82	- did a fake model's malformed tool arguments get rejected?
    83	- did the run stop at the step limit?
    84	- did Hax make a clear recommendation?
    85	
    86	It is the inspection after the flight.
    87	
    88	We need both from the first experiment, but neither requires a platform.
    89	
    90	# Language choice
    91	
    92	## Recommendation: Python
    93	
    94	Python gives HaxJobs the best combined fit for:
    95	
    96	- model provider SDKs
    97	- typed model and tool contracts through Pydantic
    98	- evaluation libraries
    99	- HTTP, HTML, PDF, data, and browser work
   100	- SQLite in the standard library
   101	- CLI development
   102	- FastAPI later
   103	- the user's existing experience
   104	- fast iteration while the product is still being discovered
   105	
   106	The harness mostly waits on model calls, web pages, browsers, and storage. Rust or Go speed would not make those remote systems answer faster.
   107	
   108	## Comparison
   109	
   110	| Question | Python | TypeScript | Go | Rust |
   111	|---|---|---|---|---|
   112	| Major model SDK support | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Thin, often REST or community clients |
   113	| Agent and evaluation libraries | Strongest current choice | Strong and improving | Limited | Limited |
   114	| Runtime validation for model data | Pydantic is a very good fit | Zod is a very good fit | More manual glue | Strong types, more schema plumbing |
   115	| Static web and document processing | Excellent | Good | Good | More work |
   116	| Browser automation | Official Playwright support | Best native Playwright experience | Community binding | Community binding |
   117	| Async network work | Good enough | Very good | Excellent | Excellent |
   118	| CPU and memory performance | Weakest | Fine | Strong | Strongest |
   119	| Native single-binary packaging | Awkward | Improving | Excellent | Excellent |
   120	| Speed for one developer building this product | Best fit | Close second | Slower | Slowest |
   121	| Later React interface | Separate API boundary | Same language | Separate API boundary | Separate API boundary |
   122	
   123	## Why TypeScript is the real alternative
   124	
   125	TypeScript has a serious case:
   126	
   127	- the web interface is already React and TypeScript
   128	- Playwright starts in the Node world
   129	- Zod gives good runtime validation
   130	- OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini support TypeScript well
   131	- one language can reduce mental switching
   132	
   133	But the one-language benefit is smaller than it looks. The browser interface and agent runtime still have different trust boundaries, dependencies, release cycles, and data access. We need runtime validation across that boundary anyway.
   134	
   135	HaxJobs' centre is model work, research, document processing, evaluation, and career data. Python wins that combination.
   136	
   137	Use TypeScript later as a thin interface over the same Python actions. Do not rewrite career logic in the browser or Node server.
   138	
   139	## Why not Go now
   140	
   141	Go would give us:
   142	
   143	- easy native binaries
   144	- simple deployment
   145	- cheap concurrency
   146	- predictable resource usage
   147	
   148	It loses on:
   149	
   150	- agent and evaluation library depth
   151	- research and document tooling
   152	- model-specific experimentation speed
   153	- the amount of glue needed around structured outputs and tests
   154	
   155	Go becomes interesting if discovery later needs thousands of concurrent fetches and profiling shows Python is the actual limit. That would justify a small fetch worker, not a rewrite of Hax.
   156	
   157	## Why not Rust now
   158	
   159	Rust would give us:
   160	
   161	- strong memory and concurrency checks
   162	- fast, small native programs
   163	- good control over resources
   164	
   165	It would also make every experiment slower to write and change. The main HaxJobs risks are bad permissions, false claims, private-data leakage, prompt injection, stale sources, and external actions without approval. Rust does not solve those.
   166	
   167	Rust becomes interesting for a constrained native component, untrusted local execution, or a hard resource target. None of those exists in Stage 0.
   168	
   169	# Exact Python floor
   170	
   171	## Python version
   172	
   173	Use a currently supported Python version that all selected dependencies support. Python 3.12 is the conservative floor. Python 3.13 is fine if the live dependency check is clean.
   174	
   175	Do not make a language-version decision from fashion. Pin the version after the Stage 0 dependency check.
   176	
   177	## Environment and packaging
   178	
   179	Use `uv` because the repository already uses it and it handles:
   180	
   181	- Python versions
   182	- virtual environments
   183	- locked dependencies
   184	- running tools
   185	- package installation
   186	
   187	No Poetry, Conda, pip-tools, or second package manager.
   188	
   189	## CLI
   190	
   191	Use the standard library's `argparse` for the experiment runner unless the command shape becomes painful.
   192	
   193	One command does not justify Typer or Click. Reconsider Typer when the real CLI has enough nested product commands that help text and typed option handling remove meaningful code.
   194	
   195	# Framework choice
   196	
   197	## Recommendation: plain Python plus Pydantic contracts
   198	
   199	The first loop is small:
   200	
   201	```python
   202	messages = build_context(fixture)
   203	response = model.complete(messages)
   204	save_trace(response)
   205	return response.text
   206	```
   207	
   208	Stage 1 adds a bounded loop:
   209	
   210	```python
   211	for step in range(max_steps):
   212	    response = model.complete(messages, tools=allowed_tools)
   213	    record_model_response(response)
   214	
   215	    if not response.tool_calls:
   216	        return response.text
   217	
   218	    messages.append(response.as_assistant_message())
   219	    for call in response.tool_calls:
   220	        result = dispatch(call)
   221	        messages.append(result.as_tool_message())
   222	```
   223	
   224	That does not need LangChain, LangGraph, PydanticAI, or the OpenAI Agents SDK yet.
   225	
   226	Pydantic should validate:
   227	
   228	- normalized model responses
   229	- tool arguments
   230	- tool results
   231	- fixture records
   232	- trace events
   233	- later product actions at trust boundaries
   234	
   235	Pydantic is a contract library here. It is not the architecture.
   236	
   237	## Why not PydanticAI from the first line
   238	
   239	PydanticAI is the strongest framework candidate for HaxJobs. It already has:
   240	
   241	- typed dependencies and outputs
   242	- broad model support
   243	- message history
   244	- toolsets
   245	- deferred tools and approvals
   246	- fake and function models for tests
   247	- OpenTelemetry instrumentation
   248	- multi-agent patterns
   249	- Temporal integration
   250	
   251	That is exactly why we should keep it nearby.
   252	
   253	But Stage 0 is supposed to expose the real primitive. If we begin inside a framework, we may learn the framework's message types and lifecycle before we learn what HaxJobs itself needs.
   254	
   255	Start plain. Switch when PydanticAI can delete tested plumbing rather than merely replacing ten obvious lines with its own vocabulary.
   256	
   257	### PydanticAI activation condition
   258	
   259	Reconsider it when the local loop has accumulated several of these:
   260

text:
261	- provider normalization
   262	- streaming
   263	- retry handling
   264	- usage limits
   265	- deferred approval continuation
   266	- message processors
   267	- toolset composition
   268	- tracing instrumentation
   269	- substantial fake-model plumbing
   270	
   271	The test is simple:
   272	
   273	> Would PydanticAI remove meaningful code while preserving HaxJobs-owned state, policy, and product actions?
   274	
   275	If yes, use it. If it only makes the code look more agent-like, do not.
   276	
   277	## OpenAI Agents SDK
   278	
   279	Good parts:
   280	
   281	- compact agent and runner model
   282	- tools, handoffs, sessions, guardrails, and tracing
   283	- strong OpenAI Responses support
   284	- multiple-provider routes exist
   285	
   286	Costs:
   287	
   288	- OpenAI concepts remain the centre
   289	- hosted tracing creates privacy and provider coupling
   290	- server-managed conversation and compaction can become hidden application state
   291	- HaxJobs would inherit provider-shaped choices too early
   292	
   293	Use it only if OpenAI Responses becomes an intentional primary platform and its realtime, hosted traces, handoffs, or server-managed state are product requirements.
   294	
   295	HaxJobs currently uses DeepSeek. That makes an OpenAI-centred framework a strange default.
   296	
   297	## LangChain
   298	
   299	LangChain is useful when an application needs many provider, retriever, and middleware integrations.
   300	
   301	HaxJobs Stage 0 needs one model call. Stage 1 needs one tool. LangChain would add more concepts than it removes.
   302	
   303	Reconsider only when we would otherwise rebuild several valuable LangChain integrations or middleware pieces.
   304	
   305	## LangGraph
   306	
   307	LangGraph is for explicit state machines with:
   308	
   309	- branches and joins
   310	- checkpointing
   311	- pause and resume
   312	- human input between nodes
   313	- replay and state inspection
   314	- subgraphs
   315	
   316	That may fit later application, research, or company-watch operations. It does not fit a one-job review yet.
   317	
   318	Reconsider when HaxJobs has a real workflow that must visibly branch and resume across restarts.
   319	
   320	## Temporal
   321	
   322	Temporal is not an agent loop. It is an outer system for durable work.
   323	
   324	It becomes useful when:
   325	
   326	- a watch sleeps for days
   327	- the process or machine can restart
   328	- an approval can arrive later
   329	- retries must survive crashes
   330	- the exact operation history matters
   331	- multiple workers execute bounded attempts
   332	
   333	That maps well to the standing commitments discussed earlier. Still, adding Temporal before one watch exists would mean operating a server to schedule nothing.
   334	
   335	Reconsider when the first standing commitment must survive process or host failure.
   336	
   337	## Prefect
   338	
   339	Prefect is a lighter Python choice for scheduled data work, retries, and an operator UI.
   340	
   341	It may fit discovery pipelines if the main need is running and observing scheduled Python work. Temporal is stronger when durable waits, approvals, and exact recovery are correctness requirements.
   342	
   343	Do not choose between them until the first real long-running operation exists.
   344	
   345	## Hermes
   346	
   347	Hermes remains a pattern library, not a runtime dependency.
   348	
   349	Useful patterns to copy in small form:
   350	
   351	- stable, contextual, and volatile prompt layers
   352	- head and recent-tail preservation during compaction
   353	- tool-result size limits
   354	- cancellation
   355	- isolated sub-agent context
   356	- SQLite session search when needed
   357	
   358	Do not import Hermes' gateway, terminal, browser, messaging, memory, provider fallback, and broad tool assumptions into HaxJobs.
   359	
   360	# Model provider boundary
   361	
   362	## One normalized boundary
   363	
   364	HaxJobs should own a small interface such as:
   365	
   366	```python
   367	class ModelClient(Protocol):
   368	    def complete(self, request: ModelRequest) -> ModelResponse:
   369	        ...
   370	```
   371	
   372	`ModelResponse` needs enough information for the run loop:
   373	
   374	- assistant text
   375	- tool calls
   376	- usage when returned
   377	- provider stop reason
   378	- provider model ID
   379	- raw provider payload or safe reference for debugging
   380	
   381	## Do not flatten providers too aggressively
   382	
   383	Providers differ in:
   384	
   385	- reasoning items
   386	- tool-call representation
   387	- conversation continuation
   388	- prompt caching
   389	- compaction
   390	- structured-output support
   391	- usage reporting
   392	
   393	The common interface should support HaxJobs without pretending the providers are identical. Provider-specific options can live in the adapter.
   394	
   395	## First provider
   396	
   397	Use the already configured real provider for Stage 0. Do not add fallback routing.
   398	
   399	Add one scripted fake that implements the same interface. The fake is more important than a second real provider because it lets us test malformed tool calls, errors, limits, and exact trajectories without network cost.
   400	
   401	# Observability floor
   402	
   403	## Recommendation: two local records
   404	
   405	### 1. Normal application log
   406	
   407	Use Python `logging` for:
   408	
   409	- warnings
   410	- exceptions
   411	- component name
   412	- run ID
   413	- timing summaries
   414	- local diagnostics
   415	
   416	Do not put full prompts, CVs, profiles, credentials, or fetched pages into logs.
   417	
   418	### 2. Agent event trace
   419	
   420	Use append-only JSONL for the experiment's structured events.
   421	
   422	One line is one event. A small trace may look like:
   423	
   424	```json
   425	{"type":"run_started","run_id":"run_123","fixture":"job-49"}
   426	{"type":"context_built","run_id":"run_123","manifest_hash":"..."}
   427	{"type":"model_completed","run_id":"run_123","model":"...","duration_ms":2800}
   428	{"type":"run_completed","run_id":"run_123","stop_reason":"final_answer"}
   429	```
   430	
   431	The real schema should include:
   432	
   433	- schema version
   434	- event, run, turn, span, and parent IDs
   435	- timestamp and duration
   436	- event type
   437	- outcome and safe error code
   438	- provider and exact model
   439	- instruction and fixture hashes
   440	- tool name and bounded safe result metadata later
   441	- usage and cost when available
   442	- stop reason
   443	
   444	## Context manifest
   445	
   446	Do not save only the final prompt string.
   447	
   448	Save identifiers and hashes for:
   449	
   450	- application revision
   451	- instruction version
   452	- flow-prompt version
   453	- model and settings
   454	- tool schema version
   455	- fixture versions
   456	- selected career-context version
   457	- redaction-policy version
   458	
   459	That lets us explain why two runs differed without copying all private content into one trace file.
   460	
   461	## Privacy rule
   462	
   463	Employment traces can contain names, addresses, work history, salary, immigration details, private documents, and provider credentials.
   464	
   465	Default rules:
   466	
   467	- no secrets
   468	- no authorization headers
   469	- no raw CV or complete profile
   470	- no arbitrary fetched-page dump
   471	- use IDs and hashes where possible
   472	- use bounded excerpts only in explicit local debug mode
   473	- redact before writing, not only before later export
   474	- sanitize any failure before turning it into a checked-in test fixture
   475	
   476	The trace store could become the biggest private-data leak in the application if we treat logging as harmless.
   477	
   478	# Why not OpenTelemetry now
   479	
   480	OpenTelemetry gives standard traces, metrics, logs, context propagation, collectors, and many backends.
   481	
   482	Stage 0 has one local process and one model call. Installing an SDK and collector would not teach us more than a clear JSONL record.
   483	
   484	Shape the local events so they can later map to spans:
   485	
   486	- run or agent span
   487	- model span
   488	- tool span
   489	- policy span
   490	- evaluator span
   491	
   492	Add OpenTelemetry when the CLI, API, worker, or background operations cross process boundaries and we need one trace across them.
   493	
   494	# OpenInference
   495	
   496	OpenInference defines AI-specific span names and fields on top of OpenTelemetry, including model, agent, tool, retriever, guardrail, and evaluator spans.
   497	
   498	Use it as vocabulary now. Do not bind business objects to its schema or install instrumentation in Stage 0.
   499	
   500	If we later add an OpenTelemetry backend, map our trace events to the useful OpenInference parts.
   501	
   502	# Trace and evaluation platforms
   503	
   504	## Phoenix
   505	
   506	Phoenix is the first platform to test if JSONL becomes annoying because it is:
   507	
   508	- open source
   509	- free to self-host
   510	- able to run without sending data to Arize
   511	- based on OpenTelemetry and OpenInference
   512	- built for model and tool traces
   513	- able to manage datasets and experiments
   514	
   515	Activation condition:
   516	
   517	- nested tool runs are hard to inspect in files
   518	- the fixture set becomes a maintained eval dataset
   519	- experiment comparison is recurring work
   520	- more than one reviewer needs searchable traces

   314	- subgraphs
   315	
   316	That may fit later application, research, or company-watch operations. It does not fit a one-job review yet.
   317	
   318	Reconsider when HaxJobs has a real workflow that must visibly branch and resume across restarts.
   319	
   320	## Temporal
   321	
   322	Temporal is not an agent loop. It is an outer system for durable work.
   323	
   324	It becomes useful when:
   325	
   326	- a watch sleeps for days
   327	- the process or machine can restart
   328	- an approval can arrive later
   329	- retries must survive crashes
   330	- the exact operation history matters
   331	- multiple workers execute bounded attempts
   332	
   333	That maps well to the standing commitments discussed earlier. Still, adding Temporal before one watch exists would mean operating a server to schedule nothing.
   334	
   335	Reconsider when the first standing commitment must survive process or host failure.
   336	
   337	## Prefect
   338	
   339	Prefect is a lighter Python choice for scheduled data work, retries, and an operator UI.
   340	
   341	It may fit discovery pipelines if the main need is running and observing scheduled Python work. Temporal is stronger when durable waits, approvals, and exact recovery are correctness requirements.
   342	
   343	Do not choose between them until the first real long-running operation exists.
   344	
   345	## Hermes
   346	
   347	Hermes remains a pattern library, not a runtime dependency.
   348	
   349	Useful patterns to copy in small form:
   350	
   351	- stable, contextual, and volatile prompt layers
   352	- head and recent-tail preservation during compaction
   353	- tool-result size limits
   354	- cancellation
   355	- isolated sub-agent context
   356	- SQLite session search when needed
   357	
   358	Do not import Hermes' gateway, terminal, browser, messaging, memory, provider fallback, and broad tool assumptions into HaxJobs.
   359	
   360	# Model provider boundary
   361	
   362	## One normalized boundary
   363	
   364	HaxJobs should own a small interface such as:
   365	
   366	```python
   367	class ModelClient(Protocol):
   368	    def complete(self, request: ModelRequest) -> ModelResponse:
   369	        ...
   370	```
   371	
   372	`ModelResponse` needs enough information for the run loop:
   373	
   374	- assistant text
   375	- tool calls
   376	- usage when returned
   377	- provider stop reason
   378	- provider model ID
   379	- raw provider payload or safe reference for debugging
   380	
   381	## Do not flatten providers too aggressively
   382	
   383	Providers differ in:
   384	
   385	- reasoning items
   386	- tool-call representation
   387	- conversation continuation
   388	- prompt caching
   389	- compaction
   390	- structured-output support
   391	- usage reporting
   392	
   393	The common interface should support HaxJobs without pretending the providers are identical. Provider-specific options can live in the adapter.
   394	
   395	## First provider
   396	
   397	Use the already configured real provider for Stage 0. Do not add fallback routing.
   398	
   399	Add one scripted fake that implements the same interface. The fake is more important than a second real provider because it lets us test malformed tool calls, errors, limits, and exact trajectories without network cost.
   400	
   401	# Observability floor
   402	
   403	## Recommendation: two local records
   404	
   405	### 1. Normal application log
   406	
   407	Use Python `logging` for:
   408	
   409	- warnings
   410	- exceptions
   411	- component name
   412	- run ID
   413	- timing summaries
   414	- local diagnostics
   415	
   416	Do not put full prompts, CVs, profiles, credentials, or fetched pages into logs.
   417	
   418	### 2. Agent event trace
   419	
   420	Use append-only JSONL for the experiment's structured events.
   421	
   422	One line is one event. A small trace may look like:
   423	
   424	```json
   425	{"type":"run_started","run_id":"run_123","fixture":"job-49"}
   426	{"type":"context_built","run_id":"run_123","manifest_hash":"..."}
   427	{"type":"model_completed","run_id":"run_123","model":"...","duration_ms":2800}
   428	{"type":"run_completed","run_id":"run_123","stop_reason":"final_answer"}
   429	```
   430	
   431	The real schema should include:
   432	
   433	- schema version
   434	- event, run, turn, span, and parent IDs
   435	- timestamp and duration
   436	- event type
   437	- outcome and safe error code
   438	- provider and exact model
   439	- instruction and fixture hashes
   440	- tool name and bounded safe result metadata later
   441	- usage and cost when available
   442	- stop reason
   443	
   444	## Context manifest
   445	
   446	Do not save only the final prompt string.
   447	
   448	Save identifiers and hashes for:
   449	
   450	- application revision
   451	- instruction version
   452	- flow-prompt version
   453	- model and settings
   454	- tool schema version
   455	- fixture versions
   456	- selected career-context version
   457	- redaction-policy version
   458	
   459	That lets us explain why two runs differed without copying all private content into one trace file.
   460	
   461	## Privacy rule
   462	
   463	Employment traces can contain names, addresses, work history, salary, immigration details, private documents, and provider credentials.
   464	
   465	Default rules:
   466	
   467	- no secrets
   468	- no authorization headers
   469	- no raw CV or complete profile
   470	- no arbitrary fetched-page dump
   471	- use IDs and hashes where possible
   472	- use bounded excerpts only in explicit local debug mode
   473	- redact before writing, not only before later export
   474	- sanitize any failure before turning it into a checked-in test fixture
   475	
   476	The trace store could become the biggest private-data leak in the application if we treat logging as harmless.
   477	
   478	# Why not OpenTelemetry now
   479	
   480	OpenTelemetry gives standard traces, metrics, logs, context propagation, collectors, and many backends.
   481	
   482	Stage 0 has one local process and one model call. Installing an SDK and collector would not teach us more than a clear JSONL record.
   483	
   484	Shape the local events so they can later map to spans:
   485	
   486	- run or agent span
   487	- model span
   488	- tool span
   489	- policy span
   490	- evaluator span
   491	
   492	Add OpenTelemetry when the CLI, API, worker, or background operations cross process boundaries and we need one trace across them.
   493	
   494	# OpenInference
   495	
   496	OpenInference defines AI-specific span names and fields on top of OpenTelemetry, including model, agent, tool, retriever, guardrail, and evaluator spans.
   497	
   498	Use it as vocabulary now. Do not bind business objects to its schema or install instrumentation in Stage 0.
   499	
   500	If we later add an OpenTelemetry backend, map our trace events to the useful OpenInference parts.
   501	
   502	# Trace and evaluation platforms
   503	
   504	## Phoenix
   505	
   506	Phoenix is the first platform to test if JSONL becomes annoying because it is:
   507	
   508	- open source
   509	- free to self-host
   510	- able to run without sending data to Arize
   511	- based on OpenTelemetry and OpenInference
   512	- built for model and tool traces
   513	- able to manage datasets and experiments
   514	
   515	Activation condition:
   516	
   517	- nested tool runs are hard to inspect in files
   518	- the fixture set becomes a maintained eval dataset
   519	- experiment comparison is recurring work
   520	- more than one reviewer needs searchable traces

text:
521	
   522	Do not deploy it before that.
   523	
   524	## Langfuse
   525	
   526	Langfuse has strong traces, prompt versions, datasets, experiments, and self-hosting.
   527	
   528	Its current full self-hosted setup includes several services such as the web app, worker, Postgres, ClickHouse, Redis or Valkey, and blob storage. That is far too much for Stage 0.
   529	
   530	Reconsider for a collaborative production setup when its prompt and evaluation workflow justifies the operations.
   531	
   532	## MLflow
   533	
   534	MLflow is open source, self-hostable, OpenTelemetry-compatible, and has tracing, evaluation, feedback, and prompt management.
   535	
   536	It is a broader ML platform than the first Hax experiment needs. Reconsider if HaxJobs later wants one open platform across model experiments, prompts, traces, and evaluation.
   537	
   538	## Logfire, LangSmith, and Braintrust
   539	
   540	These can be useful managed products, but their strongest setup routes create hosted or paid self-hosting dependencies.
   541	
   542	HaxJobs is local-first and handles private career data. They are not first choices without a deliberate data-hosting and procurement decision.
   543	
   544	# Verification floor
   545	
   546	## Layer 1: code checks with a fake model
   547	
   548	Use pytest to prove deterministic behaviour:
   549	
   550	- context ordering
   551	- fixture loading
   552	- source labels
   553	- trace creation
   554	- stop limits
   555	- unknown tool rejection
   556	- malformed argument rejection
   557	- tool result ordering
   558	- no network use in unit tests
   559	- redaction rules
   560	
   561	The fake model should be scriptable:
   562	
   563	```python
   564	fake = FakeModel([
   565	    ToolCall(name="inspect_job_source", arguments={"job_ref": "job-328"}),
   566	    FinalText("I still cannot verify enough detail to recommend this role."),
   567	])
   568	```
   569	
   570	This verifies the surrounding software without asking whether a live model happens to cooperate today.
   571	
   572	## Layer 2: fixture behaviour checks
   573	
   574	Check stable facts, not exact prose.
   575	
   576	Job 49 examples:
   577	
   578	- recommendation is negative
   579	- role family is understood as IT support
   580	- sponsorship remains unknown
   581	- cited reasons come from the supplied job evidence
   582	
   583	Job 328 examples:
   584	
   585	- evidence is marked insufficient before source inspection
   586	- no hidden old-evaluation claims appear
   587	- the next useful check is source inspection
   588	
   589	Do not snapshot the full answer. Natural language will vary and exact snapshots will fail for harmless wording changes.
   590	
   591	## Layer 3: human rubric
   592	
   593	A person reads the live answer and records:
   594	
   595	- grounded or invented
   596	- clear or vague
   597	- useful recommendation or fence-sitting
   598	- natural Hax voice or generic report
   599	- correct handling of unknowns
   600	- any harmful or misleading advice
   601	
   602	For Stage 0, the user and developer are the same person, so this can be one short Markdown review per run.
   603	
   604	## Layer 4: later model grader
   605	
   606	A model grader may later help score qualities such as clarity, groundedness, or tone across many cases.
   607	
   608	It must not become the release authority for:
   609	
   610	- factual correctness
   611	- consent
   612	- external actions
   613	- private-data handling
   614	- evidence provenance
   615	
   616	Model judges have position and style biases. If we add one, pin the judge model and rubric, compare against human labels, shuffle answer order for pairwise tests, and keep disagreement visible.
   617	
   618	# Evaluation tools
   619	
   620	## pytest
   621	
   622	Use now. It handles the deterministic floor and already fits Python.
   623	
   624	## promptfoo
   625	
   626	Useful later for:
   627	
   628	- comparing several prompts or models
   629	- deterministic tool-use assertions
   630	- red-team matrices
   631	- latency and cost comparisons
   632	
   633	It adds Node tooling. Add it only when the comparison matrix becomes painful in pytest.
   634	
   635	## DeepEval
   636	
   637	Useful for ready-made model-graded metrics. Most interesting metrics still depend on a judge model.
   638	
   639	Do not add it until one specific metric has been checked against HaxJobs human labels.
   640	
   641	## Inspect AI
   642	
   643	Useful for serious agent evaluations, sandboxed tasks, rich logs, datasets, and scorers.
   644	
   645	It is a good later option for adversarial or long-running capability tests. It is too much for two initial job fixtures.
   646	
   647	## Pydantic Evals
   648	
   649	Worth reconsidering with PydanticAI or once our fixture evaluation code starts becoming its own small framework. Plain pytest is enough first.
   650	
   651	# Recommended first stack
   652	
   653	```text
   654	Runtime:          Python 3.12 or 3.13, pinned after dependency check
   655	Environment:      uv
   656	Contracts:        Pydantic v2
   657	Agent loop:       plain Python
   658	Provider:         current configured provider through one local adapter
   659	Test model:       one scripted fake adapter
   660	CLI:              argparse
   661	HTTP later:       ordinary bounded HTTP client
   662	Storage Stage 0:  local JSONL run artifacts
   663	Diagnostics:      Python logging
   664	Verification:     pytest + fixture checks + Markdown human rubric
   665	Web UI later:     React + TypeScript calling Python product actions
   666	```
   667	
   668	# Activation table
   669	
   670	| Observed need | Add or reconsider |
   671	|---|---|
   672	| Loop plumbing becomes a maintained subsystem | PydanticAI |
   673	| OpenAI becomes the deliberate main platform | OpenAI Agents SDK |
   674	| Explicit branched workflows need checkpoint, pause, resume, and replay | LangGraph |
   675	| Standing commitments must survive host failure and wait for days | Temporal |
   676	| Main need is scheduled Python pipelines with an operator UI | Prefect |
   677	| Local JSONL is painful to inspect or compare | Phoenix |
   678	| CLI, API, and worker need one cross-process trace | OpenTelemetry |
   679	| Prompt and model comparison matrix outgrows pytest | promptfoo or Pydantic Evals |
   680	| Adversarial long-horizon agent tests need sandboxes and rich transcripts | Inspect AI |
   681	| Python is proven to bottleneck high-volume source fetching | A small Go fetch service |
   682	| Constrained native or untrusted execution becomes real | A narrow Rust component |
   683	
   684	# Main tradeoff in the recommendation
   685	
   686	Owning the first loop means we write and test some plumbing ourselves.
   687	
   688	The benefit is that we see exactly how instructions, context, tools, traces, and stops work. That matters because HaxJobs is supposed to be a job-native agent system, not an application hidden inside another agent framework.
   689	
   690	The cost stays acceptable only while the loop remains small. We should not become emotionally attached to custom code. If PydanticAI later removes a growing pile of provider and lifecycle plumbing without taking ownership of HaxJobs policy or state, switching is the lazy and correct move.
   691	
   692	# Research sources
   693	
   694	Primary references used for this decision:
   695	
   696	- [OpenAI API libraries](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/libraries)
   697	- [Anthropic client SDKs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/client-sdks)
   698	- [Gemini API libraries](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/libraries)
   699	- [Python asyncio](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html)
   700	- [Python sqlite3](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sqlite3.html)
   701	- [Pydantic JSON Schema](https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/api/pydantic/json_schema/)
   702	- [PydanticAI testing](https://pydantic.dev/docs/ai/guides/testing/)
   703	- [PydanticAI deferred tools](https://pydantic.dev/docs/ai/tools-toolsets/deferred-tools/)
   704	- [PydanticAI Temporal integration](https://pydantic.dev/docs/ai/integrations/durable_execution/temporal/)
   705	- [OpenAI Agents SDK](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/)
   706	- [LangGraph persistence](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/persistence)
   707	- [Temporal Python SDK](https://docs.temporal.io/develop/python)
   708	- [Prefect interactive workflows](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/advanced/interactive)
   709	- [OpenTelemetry sensitive-data guidance](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/security/handling-sensitive-data/)
   710	- [OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/)
   711	- [OpenInference specification](https://arize-ai.github.io/openinference/spec/)
   712	- [Phoenix self-hosting](https://arize.com/docs/phoenix/self-hosting)
   713	- [Langfuse self-hosting](https://langfuse.com/self-hosting)
   714	- [MLflow tracing](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/genai/tracing/)
   715	- [Promptfoo deterministic assertions](https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/expected-outputs/deterministic/)
   716	- [Inspect AI evaluation logs](https://inspect.aisi.org.uk/eval-logs.html)
   717	
   718	# Questions for Arinze
   719	
   720	1. **Language split:** Decided. Python owns Hax and career logic. TypeScript is reserved for the later interface.
   721	2. **Framework floor:** Decided. Stage 0 and Stage 1 use plain Python plus Pydantic. PydanticAI is the first framework to reconsider when it can delete meaningful loop code.
   722	3. **Evidence stack:** Decided. The first experiments use local redacted JSONL, Python logging, pytest, and Markdown human review, with no telemetry or evaluation server.
   723	
   724	# Decision ledger
   725	
   726	| Item | Status | Current direction |
   727	|---|---|---|
   728	| Core language | Decided | Python. |
   729	| Later web language | Decided | TypeScript as an interface only. |
   730	| Environment | Decided | `uv`. |
   731	| Contract library | Decided | Pydantic v2. |
   732	| First run loop | Decided | Plain Python. |
   733	| First provider strategy | Working recommendation | One configured provider plus one scripted fake. |
   734	| CLI | Working recommendation | `argparse` until command complexity justifies more. |
   735	| Stage 0 traces | Decided | Local redacted JSONL plus Python logging. |
   736	| Stage 0 verification | Decided | pytest, fixture checks, and Markdown human review. |
   737	| First framework to reconsider | Working recommendation | PydanticAI. |
   738	| First trace UI to reconsider | Working recommendation | Phoenix. |
   739	| Durable workflow system | Deferred | Compare Temporal and Prefect when a standing commitment must survive process failure. |
   740	| Go or Rust | Deferred | Add only as narrow measured components, not Hax's core. |

   706	- [LangGraph persistence](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/persistence)
   707	- [Temporal Python SDK](https://docs.temporal.io/develop/python)
   708	- [Prefect interactive workflows](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/advanced/interactive)
   709	- [OpenTelemetry sensitive-data guidance](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/security/handling-sensitive-data/)
   710	- [OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/)
   711	- [OpenInference specification](https://arize-ai.github.io/openinference/spec/)
   712	- [Phoenix self-hosting](https://arize.com/docs/phoenix/self-hosting)
   713	- [Langfuse self-hosting](https://langfuse.com/self-hosting)
   714	- [MLflow tracing](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/genai/tracing/)
   715	- [Promptfoo deterministic assertions](https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/expected-outputs/deterministic/)
   716	- [Inspect AI evaluation logs](https://inspect.aisi.org.uk/eval-logs.html)
   717	
   718	# Questions for Arinze
   719	
   720	1. **Language split:** Decided. Python owns Hax and career logic. TypeScript is reserved for the later interface.
   721	2. **Framework floor:** Decided. Stage 0 and Stage 1 use plain Python plus Pydantic. PydanticAI is the first framework to reconsider when it can delete meaningful loop code.
   722	3. **Evidence stack:** Decided. The first experiments use local redacted JSONL, Python logging, pytest, and Markdown human review, with no telemetry or evaluation server.
   723	
   724	# Decision ledger
   725	
   726	| Item | Status | Current direction |
   727	|---|---|---|
   728	| Core language | Decided | Python. |
   729	| Later web language | Decided | TypeScript as an interface only. |
   730	| Environment | Decided | `uv`. |
   731	| Contract library | Decided | Pydantic v2. |
   732	| First run loop | Decided | Plain Python. |
   733	| First provider strategy | Working recommendation | One configured provider plus one scripted fake. |
   734	| CLI | Working recommendation | `argparse` until command complexity justifies more. |
   735	| Stage 0 traces | Decided | Local redacted JSONL plus Python logging. |
   736	| Stage 0 verification | Decided | pytest, fixture checks, and Markdown human review. |
   737	| First framework to reconsider | Working recommendation | PydanticAI. |
   738	| First trace UI to reconsider | Working recommendation | Phoenix. |
   739	| Durable workflow system | Deferred | Compare Temporal and Prefect when a standing commitment must survive process failure. |
   740	| Go or Rust | Deferred | Add only as narrow measured components, not Hax's core. |

text:
1	---
     2	status: discussing
     3	created: 2026-07-17
     4	scope: Decide how HaxJobs should mirror Pi's architecture while adding employment-native tools and controlled coding capabilities
     5	builds_on:
     6	  - discussion/004-minimal-job-native-harness.md
     7	  - discussion/005-implementation-stack-observability-and-verification.md
     8	studied_systems:
     9	  - Pi commit 3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21
    10	  - Hermes commit 9e1b1d7536270b4e2bf56662903acfbfc54ac937
    11	---
    12	
    13	# Pi-inspired HaxJobs architecture
    14	
    15	## The user's correction
    16	
    17	HaxJobs should not study coding-agent systems only to learn a generic model and tool loop.
    18	
    19	A serious employment agent will need many capabilities that coding agents already solved:
    20	
    21	- reading user-selected files
    22	- searching folders and project repositories
    23	- writing and editing artifacts
    24	- running code and tests when helping the user build evidence projects
    25	- streaming progress
    26	- cancelling work
    27	- maintaining conversations
    28	- selecting tools per operation
    29	- keeping tool results together with model history
    30	- compacting long conversations
    31	- observing runs
    32	- loading procedures only when relevant
    33	- isolating specialist work later
    34	
    35	HaxJobs is not a coding agent, but helping someone become employable can include real coding work. If Hax designs a missing portfolio project with the user, then cannot inspect, edit, run, and verify that project, the employability promise stops halfway.
    36	
    37	So the right answer is not "career tools instead of coding tools."
    38	
    39	It is:
    40	
    41	> Employment-native tools are the main product surface. Coding tools become a first-class scoped capability when the employment work genuinely enters a project or document workspace.
    42	
    43	# What Pi actually separates
    44	
    45	The current Pi repository has a cleaner split than the older coding-agent-only picture.
    46	
    47	```text
    48	pi-ai
    49	  provider and model boundary
    50	
    51	pi-agent-core
    52	  messages, model/tool loop, events, cancellation, tool validation
    53	
    54	pi-coding-agent
    55	  coding prompt, filesystem and shell tools, resources, sessions, extensions, CLI and TUI
    56	```
    57	
    58	The important lesson is that `read`, `bash`, `edit`, and `write` are not baked into the generic model loop. They belong to Pi's coding-specific product layer.
    59	
    60	Pi's low-level loop only understands:
    61	
    62	- messages
    63	- a system prompt
    64	- active tool definitions
    65	- a model stream
    66	- tool calls and results
    67	- lifecycle events
    68	- stop, cancellation, steering, and follow-up conditions
    69	
    70	Source: [Pi agent loop](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/agent/src/agent-loop.ts#L155-L318).
    71	
    72	Pi's coding layer then registers seven concrete tools and chooses an active subset:
    73	
    74	- `read`
    75	- `bash`
    76	- `edit`
    77	- `write`
    78	- `grep`
    79	- `find`
    80	- `ls`
    81	
    82	Source: [Pi coding tool registry](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/coding-agent/src/core/tools/index.ts#L50-L155).
    83	
    84	Pi currently enables `read`, `bash`, `edit`, and `write` by default for an attended coding session. Explicit tool lists, no-tools modes, and exclusions alter the active set.
    85	
    86	Source: [Pi session creation](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/coding-agent/src/core/sdk.ts#L215-L225).
    87	
    88	That separation is exactly what HaxJobs needs.
    89	
    90	# Proposed HaxJobs mapping
    91	
    92	Keep one Python package initially. Mirror Pi's conceptual boundaries without creating a package monorepo before we need one.
    93	
    94	```text
    95	Hax model boundary
    96	  provider adapters and normalized model responses
    97	
    98	Hax agent core
    99	  internal messages, bounded loop, tools, events, cancellation, save points
   100	
   101	Hax employment layer
   102	  career context, evidence rules, employment tools, capability selection, approvals
   103	
   104	Interfaces
   105	  CLI first, web later, worker later
   106	```
   107	
   108	## 1. Model boundary
   109	
   110	Equivalent to Pi's `pi-ai`, but much smaller at first.
   111	
   112	Responsibilities:
   113	
   114	- one `ModelClient` protocol
   115	- one DeepSeek-compatible adapter
   116	- one scripted fake
   117	- normalized text, tool calls, stop reason, usage, and provider metadata
   118	- provider-specific options kept inside the adapter
   119	
   120	No product logic.
   121	
   122	## 2. Agent core
   123	
   124	Equivalent to Pi's `pi-agent-core`.
   125	
   126	Responsibilities:
   127	
   128	- internal message types
   129	- model message projection
   130	- bounded model to tool to model loop
   131	- tool argument validation
   132	- tool-result ordering
   133	- cancellation
   134	- lifecycle events
   135	- step limits
   136	- future steering and follow-up
   137	- save points between turns
   138	
   139	No CV rules. No fit scoring. No company logic. No application logic.
   140	
   141	## 3. Employment layer
   142	
   143	Equivalent to Pi's `pi-coding-agent`, but centred on getting the user hired.
   144	
   145	Responsibilities:
   146	
   147	- Hax identity and employment instructions
   148	- career-context assembly
   149	- evidence provenance
   150	- job and company context
   151	- employment-native tools
   152	- workspace selection
   153	- tool-capability selection
   154	- external-action approvals
   155	- later sessions, skills, commands, and operation types
   156	
   157	This is where HaxJobs differs from Pi.
   158	
   159	## 4. Interfaces
   160	
   161	The CLI, later web interface, and future worker call the same employment agent and product actions.
   162	
   163	They do not implement separate tool loops or career logic.
   164	
   165	# What to copy from Pi now
   166	
   167	## Internal messages are not provider messages
   168	
   169	Pi keeps application messages separate from the final provider-compatible messages. A projection step decides what the model sees.
   170	
   171	Source: [Pi message flow](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/agent/README.md#L16-L35).
   172	
   173	HaxJobs needs this even more.
   174	
   175	An internal record may contain:
   176	
   177	- a user message
   178	- an assistant response
   179	- a tool request
   180	- a tool result
   181	- a source observation
   182	- an approval state
   183	- a saved user decision
   184	- an operational event
   185	- a private evidence reference
   186	
   187	Not all of those should be copied into the next model prompt. The context assembler produces the model-visible view.
   188	
   189	## Tool definitions are separate from active tools
   190	
   191	Pi can know about all seven coding tools while exposing only the current active subset.
   192	
   193	HaxJobs should know about its full tool catalogue while selecting capabilities for the current operation.
   194	
   195	That means:
   196	
   197	```text
   198	registered tools != tools visible to this model turn
   199	```
   200	
   201	The model can reason freely. The runtime controls what effects are possible.
   202	
   203	## Tool calls are validated before execution
   204	
   205	Pi resolves the named tool, validates arguments, runs a pre-tool policy hook, executes, catches failure, then returns a tool-result message.
   206	
   207	Source: [Pi tool preparation and execution](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/agent/src/agent-loop.ts#L519-L675).
   208	
   209	HaxJobs should mirror this ordering:
   210	
   211	```text
   212	model requests action
   213	-> find registered tool
   214	-> validate Pydantic arguments
   215	-> check capability and approval policy
   216	-> execute normal Python action
   217	-> validate and redact result
   218	-> append result
   219	-> continue or stop
   220	```
   221	
   222	## Save points between model turns
   223	
   224	Pi's higher harness persists completed messages and pending writes, emits a save point, rebuilds the next turn snapshot, then allows another provider request.
   225	
   226	Source: [Pi save-point handling](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/agent/src/harness/agent-harness.ts#L438-L542).
   227	
   228	This prevents mid-turn changes from silently changing the request already in flight.
   229	
   230	HaxJobs should eventually use the same rule:

   213	-> find registered tool

text:
231	
   232	- one model request sees one frozen turn snapshot
   233	- profile, tool, approval, or context changes affect the next model call
   234	- completed effects are saved before another call starts
   235	
   236	Stage 0 has one call, so the save-point design stays small.
   237	
   238	## Events are stable and passive
   239	
   240	Pi distinguishes lifecycle observation from policy hooks. Its observability design says the core emits safe events and adapters decide whether they become JSON, OpenTelemetry, Sentry, or something else.
   241	
   242	Source: [Pi observability design](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/agent/docs/observability.md#L1-L77).
   243	
   244	That supports the JSONL decision already made for HaxJobs.
   245	
   246	The rule should be:
   247	
   248	- observers record what happened
   249	- policy checks can block or change an action
   250	- an observer failure must not change the agent's work
   251	
   252	## Fake-provider tests
   253	
   254	Pi tests the loop and harness using scripted provider responses rather than real model calls.
   255	
   256	HaxJobs already decided on the same pattern. This is one of the clearest things to mirror.
   257	
   258	# What to copy from Hermes
   259	
   260	Pi is the better shape for the small core. Hermes has useful Python implementation lessons.
   261	
   262	## Prepare the turn, then run the loop
   263	
   264	Hermes separates once-per-turn setup from repeated model and tool execution through a turn-context boundary.
   265	
   266	Useful later when Hax has:
   267	
   268	- session loading
   269	- career-context selection
   270	- memory retrieval
   271	- prompt assembly
   272	- provider settings
   273	- capability selection
   274	
   275	Do not create a large `TurnContext` in Stage 0. Keep the seam in mind as these inputs appear.
   276	
   277	## Canonical messages, temporary provider overlays
   278	
   279	Hermes keeps one canonical history and injects temporary memory or plugin context only into the provider request copy.
   280	
   281	Source: [Hermes conversation context copy](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/blob/9e1b1d7536270b4e2bf56662903acfbfc54ac937/agent/conversation_loop.py#L849-L928).
   282	
   283	HaxJobs should use this for selected profile evidence and current job context. Retrieved context belongs on the current model desk. It does not automatically become permanent conversation truth.
   284	
   285	## Strict tool registry
   286	
   287	Hermes' useful registry core is name, schema, handler, toolset, availability, dispatch, and one result contract.
   288	
   289	Source: [Hermes tool registry](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/blob/9e1b1d7536270b4e2bf56662903acfbfc54ac937/tools/registry.py#L84-L113).
   290	
   291	Do not copy AST discovery, dynamic schema mutation, dozens of toolsets, or plugin shadowing yet.
   292	
   293	## Side-effect approvals belong below prompts
   294	
   295	Hermes places approval checks around actual execution. HaxJobs needs a stricter employment version.
   296	
   297	A prompt saying "ask first" is not an approval system.
   298	
   299	Application submission, outreach sending, connections, and external representation must stop at the action boundary until the exact action is approved.
   300	
   301	# Coding tools in HaxJobs
   302	
   303	The user's point stands: HaxJobs should have coding tools.
   304	
   305	The key decision is where they operate.
   306	
   307	## Career state is not a general filesystem
   308	
   309	The canonical career profile, decisions, applications, evidence claims, and commitments should be changed through typed employment actions.
   310	
   311	A generic `write` call must not rewrite the career database or profile files behind those actions.
   312	
   313	Why:
   314	
   315	- it bypasses validation
   316	- it bypasses provenance
   317	- it bypasses history
   318	- it bypasses approval
   319	- it can make durable state contradict itself
   320	
   321	## User document workspace
   322	
   323	Hax may need to inspect:
   324	
   325	- CVs
   326	- cover letters
   327	- certificates
   328	- transcripts
   329	- job descriptions
   330	- common application answers
   331	- portfolio material
   332	- exported LinkedIn data
   333	
   334	Recommended capability:
   335	
   336	- `read`
   337	- `ls`
   338	- `find`
   339	- perhaps `grep`
   340	- domain parsers such as `extract_document`
   341	
   342	The workspace should be made from files the user selected or uploaded. It should not mean unrestricted access to the user's home directory.
   343	
   344	Writing should happen through explicit artifact actions or a selected output directory.
   345	
   346	## Employability project workspace
   347	
   348	Hax may help the user design and complete a real project that creates missing evidence.
   349	
   350	Recommended capability inside a selected project root:
   351	
   352	- `read`
   353	- `grep`
   354	- `find`
   355	- `ls`
   356	- `write`
   357	- `edit`
   358	- `bash`
   359	
   360	This is the Pi-like coding mode inside HaxJobs.
   361	
   362	It is still employment work because the project's purpose, target skill, evidence goal, and completion proof come from the user's employability plan.
   363	
   364	## Why `bash` is different
   365	
   366	`bash` can bypass almost every tool restriction:
   367	
   368	- read any accessible file
   369	- change the career database
   370	- access credentials
   371	- call external services
   372	- send data
   373	- install software
   374	- execute generated code
   375	
   376	A path check inside the bash tool is not enough.
   377	
   378	For attended local use, bash can initially require explicit project-workspace activation and clear disclosure.
   379	
   380	For unattended, automated, or cloud use, bash must run inside an OS or container boundary with:
   381	
   382	- only the selected project mounted
   383	- minimum credentials
   384	- controlled network access
   385	- time, output, process, and disk limits
   386	
   387	Pi itself says its project trust is not a sandbox and recommends real containers or VMs for untrusted or unattended work.
   388	
   389	Source: [Pi security model](https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/3da591ab74ab9ab407e72ed882600b2c851fae21/packages/coding-agent/docs/security.md#L1-L57).
   390	
   391	## Proposed capability sets
   392	
   393	These are permission bundles, not rigid intent labels. One user interaction may create several operations with different bundles.
   394	
   395	| Capability set | Likely tools | Purpose |
   396	|---|---|---|
   397	| Conversation | None or selected read-only career context | Discuss, clarify, explain |
   398	| Career records | Typed profile, evidence, track, decision, and outcome actions | Read or change canonical career state |
   399	| Job research | Search, fetch, inspect source, company and job actions | Verify external employment information |
   400	| Document intake | Read-only selected-file tools and document extraction | Understand user-provided material |
   401	| Application preparation | Typed pack and artifact actions | Create controlled application material |
   402	| Project workspace | Pi-like filesystem tools plus bash inside a selected root | Build and verify employability evidence |
   403	| External effect | Submit, send, connect, publish | Exact approval required every time |
   404	
   405	The model does not receive every tool because they exist. The operation receives the capabilities needed for its work.
   406	
   407	# Initial recommendation per coding tool
   408	
   409	| Tool | Long-term place in HaxJobs | Initial boundary |
   410	|---|---|---|
   411	| `read` | Document and project workspaces | User-selected roots only, canonical paths resolved |
   412	| `grep` | Document collections and project repositories | Read-only, bounded results |
   413	| `find` | Selected workspaces | Read-only, ignore rules, bounded results |
   414	| `ls` | Selected workspaces | Read-only, no home-directory enumeration |
   415	| `write` | Project workspace and explicit artifact output | Selected root, no career-state internals |
   416	| `edit` | Project workspace and controlled text artifacts | Selected root, exact replacement, mutation history |
   417	| `bash` | Employability project workspace | Explicit activation; sandbox required before unattended use |
   418	
   419	None of these should be added to Stage 0 or the Job 49 review. The first experiment is still deliberately tool-free.
   420	
   421	Stage 1 still adds only `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` because we are measuring one change.
   422	
   423	The coding tools enter when we test document intake or project building.
   424	
   425	# Native employment tools remain primary
   426	
   427	Coding tools manipulate generic files and processes. They cannot replace job-native actions.
   428	
   429	Examples of native actions Hax may eventually need:
   430	
   431	- inspect a job source
   432	- search jobs across approved sources
   433	- research a company
   434	- retrieve relevant career evidence
   435	- assess fit
   436	- explain missing evidence
   437	- propose an evidence project
   438	- record a user decision
   439	- prepare application material
   440	- track an application
   441	- prepare interview practice
   442	- update an outcome
   443	- maintain a company watch
   444	
   445	The exact catalogue should still come from real runs. This note only settles the relationship:
   446	
   447	```text
   448	employment actions express product meaning
   449	coding tools provide controlled workspace capability
   450	```
   451	
   452	# Commands, skills, extensions, and workflows
   453	
   454	Pi gives useful distinctions, but HaxJobs should keep them small.
   455	
   456	## Command
   457	
   458	An explicit interface entry point, such as reviewing a fixture or opening a project workspace.
   459	
   460	Commands start work. They do not contain business logic.

text:
461	
   462	## Tool
   463	
   464	One bounded action callable by the model.
   465	
   466	Tools have typed arguments, typed results, capability requirements, and side-effect metadata.
   467	
   468	## Workflow
   469	
   470	Controller-owned order that must be followed for correctness or safety.
   471	
   472	If an application must be verified, approved, then submitted exactly once, that ordering belongs in code rather than a skill prompt.
   473	
   474	## Skill
   475	
   476	A progressively loaded procedure that helps the model perform a repeated kind of work.
   477	
   478	Pi puts skill metadata in the prompt and loads full instructions only when selected. HaxJobs can mirror that when repeated employment procedures actually exist.
   479	
   480	## Extension
   481	
   482	A way to add tools, hooks, resources, or interfaces without changing the core.
   483	
   484	Do not build a third-party extension system yet. Native Python registration is enough. Connectors such as Gmail or WhatsApp may later justify a plugin boundary.
   485	
   486	# What not to mirror from Pi
   487	
   488	## Do not copy the permissive default
   489	
   490	Pi is an attended local coding agent. It runs with the user's permissions and explicitly says project trust is not a sandbox.
   491	
   492	HaxJobs may later operate unattended and hold private career data. Its capability defaults must be narrower.
   493	
   494	## Do not copy the JSONL conversation tree as career storage
   495	
   496	Pi's JSONL session tree is good for conversation branching and replay.
   497	
   498	Career evidence, jobs, decisions, commitments, applications, and outcomes need their own durable records. A conversation transcript is not the career model.
   499	
   500	Stage 0 JSONL remains a run trace, not the final product database.
   501	
   502	## Do not build a plugin marketplace
   503	
   504	Pi's extension system supports custom tools, commands, providers, UI, resource loading, and event interception. HaxJobs has one developer and no proven third-party extension need.
   505	
   506	## Do not copy coding prompt text
   507	
   508	Keep Pi's assembly pattern, not its coding identity, file instructions, or project assumptions.
   509	
   510	## Do not treat Pi's design documents as shipped behaviour
   511	
   512	At the studied commit, parts of Pi's generic hooks, automatic compaction, crash recovery, and observability package remain planned or incomplete.
   513	
   514	We should copy verified runtime patterns and use design notes as ideas, not dependencies.
   515	
   516	# Proposed smallest architecture for Stage 0 and Stage 1
   517	
   518	```text
   519	experiment CLI
   520	    |
   521	    v
   522	EmploymentAgent
   523	    |-- context builder
   524	    |-- Hax instructions
   525	    |-- active capability selection
   526	    |-- AgentLoop
   527	    |      |-- ModelClient
   528	    |      |-- tool validation and dispatch
   529	    |      |-- lifecycle events
   530	    |
   531	    |-- JSONL event sink
   532	    |-- run result
   533	```
   534	
   535	Stage 0 active tools:
   536	
   537	```text
   538	[]
   539	```
   540	
   541	Stage 1 active tools:
   542	
   543	```text
   544	[inspect_job_source]
   545	```
   546	
   547	The registry may know only what each stage needs. We do not have to register the future catalogue before building it.
   548	
   549	# Recommended architectural decisions
   550	
   551	1. Mirror Pi's provider core, agent core, domain layer, and interface separation conceptually inside the Python package.
   552	2. Keep one HaxJobs loop rather than separate loops for CLI, web, and worker.
   553	3. Keep internal messages separate from provider messages.
   554	4. Separate registered tools from active tools.
   555	5. Select tools through capability sets attached to work, not one global all-tools list.
   556	6. Treat native employment actions as canonical product operations.
   557	7. Add Pi-like coding tools for selected document and employability project workspaces.
   558	8. Keep generic writes and bash away from canonical career storage.
   559	9. Require a real sandbox before bash can run unattended or in the cloud.
   560	10. Copy Pi's save points, event ordering, fake-provider test shape, and passive-observability rule in small form.
   561	11. Copy Hermes' canonical-history, temporary-context, strict-registry, and side-effect-approval ideas.
   562	12. Delay compaction, extensions, delegation, skills, and durable workflow machinery until real runs demand them.
   563	
   564	# Questions for Arinze
   565	
   566	1. **Architecture shape:** Should HaxJobs mirror Pi's provider core, agent core, employment layer, and interface split, while keeping them as modules inside one Python package for now? My recommendation is yes.
   567	2. **Coding workspace:** Should building real employability projects be a first-class Hax capability with `read`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash` inside an explicitly selected project root? My recommendation is yes.
   568	3. **Document workspace:** Should user-selected intake files receive read-only file tools, while profile and career-state changes continue through typed employment actions? My recommendation is yes.
   569	4. **Shell boundary:** Should `bash` require explicit project-workspace activation now and a real container or VM boundary before unattended or cloud execution? My recommendation is yes.
   570	
   571	# Decision ledger
   572	
   573	| Item | Status | Current direction |
   574	|---|---|---|
   575	| Pi as architecture reference | Open | Recommend mirroring its layered separation and small loop philosophy. |
   576	| Hermes as architecture reference | Working recommendation | Borrow selected Python boundaries, not the full runtime. |
   577	| Physical package split | Open | Keep one Python package initially. |
   578	| Coding tools in HaxJobs | Open | Include as a scoped project-workspace capability. |
   579	| Document file tools | Open | Read-only access to user-selected roots. |
   580	| Career-state mutation | Working recommendation | Typed employment actions only. |
   581	| Tool activation | Working recommendation | Registered catalogue plus per-operation active capability set. |
   582	| Bash | Open | Explicit project activation; OS isolation before unattended use. |
   583	| Stage 0 tools | Decided | None. |
   584	| Stage 1 tools | Decided | `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` only. |
   585	| Generic plugin system | Deferred | Native Python registration first. |
   586	| Session tree, compaction, sub-agents, skills | Deferred | Add from observed needs. |
   587	
   588	# Converged technical reference
   589	
   590	The three disposable source studies have been merged into one durable implementation reference:
   591	
   592	- [Pi and Hermes study for a job-native HaxJobs architecture](research/2026-07-17-pi-hermes-job-native-harness-study.md)
   593	
   594	It keeps the detailed runtime findings, exact source links, shipped-versus-planned warnings, coding-tool analysis, HaxJobs mapping, risks, planning invariants, and likely implementation sequence. Read it with this decision note before writing an execution plan.

   529	    |      |-- lifecycle events
   530	    |
   531	    |-- JSONL event sink
   532	    |-- run result
   533	```
   534	
   535	Stage 0 active tools:
   536	
   537	```text
   538	[]
   539	```
   540	
   541	Stage 1 active tools:
   542	
   543	```text
   544	[inspect_job_source]
   545	```
   546	
   547	The registry may know only what each stage needs. We do not have to register the future catalogue before building it.
   548	
   549	# Recommended architectural decisions
   550	
   551	1. Mirror Pi's provider core, agent core, domain layer, and interface separation conceptually inside the Python package.
   552	2. Keep one HaxJobs loop rather than separate loops for CLI, web, and worker.
   553	3. Keep internal messages separate from provider messages.
   554	4. Separate registered tools from active tools.
   555	5. Select tools through capability sets attached to work, not one global all-tools list.
   556	6. Treat native employment actions as canonical product operations.
   557	7. Add Pi-like coding tools for selected document and employability project workspaces.
   558	8. Keep generic writes and bash away from canonical career storage.
   559	9. Require a real sandbox before bash can run unattended or in the cloud.
   560	10. Copy Pi's save points, event ordering, fake-provider test shape, and passive-observability rule in small form.
   561	11. Copy Hermes' canonical-history, temporary-context, strict-registry, and side-effect-approval ideas.
   562	12. Delay compaction, extensions, delegation, skills, and durable workflow machinery until real runs demand them.
   563	
   564	# Questions for Arinze
   565	
   566	1. **Architecture shape:** Should HaxJobs mirror Pi's provider core, agent core, employment layer, and interface split, while keeping them as modules inside one Python package for now? My recommendation is yes.
   567	2. **Coding workspace:** Should building real employability projects be a first-class Hax capability with `read`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash` inside an explicitly selected project root? My recommendation is yes.
   568	3. **Document workspace:** Should user-selected intake files receive read-only file tools, while profile and career-state changes continue through typed employment actions? My recommendation is yes.
   569	4. **Shell boundary:** Should `bash` require explicit project-workspace activation now and a real container or VM boundary before unattended or cloud execution? My recommendation is yes.
   570	
   571	# Decision ledger
   572	
   573	| Item | Status | Current direction |
   574	|---|---|---|
   575	| Pi as architecture reference | Open | Recommend mirroring its layered separation and small loop philosophy. |
   576	| Hermes as architecture reference | Working recommendation | Borrow selected Python boundaries, not the full runtime. |
   577	| Physical package split | Open | Keep one Python package initially. |
   578	| Coding tools in HaxJobs | Open | Include as a scoped project-workspace capability. |
   579	| Document file tools | Open | Read-only access to user-selected roots. |
   580	| Career-state mutation | Working recommendation | Typed employment actions only. |
   581	| Tool activation | Working recommendation | Registered catalogue plus per-operation active capability set. |
   582	| Bash | Open | Explicit project activation; OS isolation before unattended use. |
   583	| Stage 0 tools | Decided | None. |
   584	| Stage 1 tools | Decided | `inspect_job_source(job_ref)` only. |
   585	| Generic plugin system | Deferred | Native Python registration first. |
   586	| Session tree, compaction, sub-agents, skills | Deferred | Add from observed needs. |
   587	
   588	# Converged technical reference
   589	
   590	The three disposable source studies have been merged into one durable implementation reference:
   591	
   592	- [Pi and Hermes study for a job-native HaxJobs architecture](research/2026-07-17-pi-hermes-job-native-harness-study.md)
   593	
   594	It keeps the detailed runtime findings, exact source links, shipped-versus-planned warnings, coding-tool analysis, HaxJobs mapping, risks, planning invariants, and likely implementation sequence. Read it with this decision note before writing an execution plan.
